observations.recommendation: We recommended the following: Direct the Municipal Accountant to secure the available documents and information pertaining to the Due from Local Government Units account balance and determine whether these are sufficient to assert validity of claim of LGU Cabatuan, Isabela. Thereupon, issue Demand Letters to the concerned local government units and exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables; Instruct the Municipal Accountant to verify, analyze, and validate the existence of the receivables and if appropriate, secure the necessary documents to support the request for write-off of the dormant receivables amounting to ₱1.202 million and follow the procedures for the proper disposition of dormant accounts prescribed by COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to correct the balance of Due from Local Government Units account and record the procurement and audit distribution of the Construction Materials for Distribution amounting to ₱9.322 million in the respective books of the municipality and the barangays; and Direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize Allowance for Impairment on the remaining Due from Local Government Units balance of ₱1.353 million pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented. The concerned office/personnel expressed their intention to implement the action plan to satisfy the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Needs further monitoring and evaluation

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure the timely and full implementation of the projects downloaded by national government agencies, cause the immediate liquidation of the funds transferred, and return the unexpended balances to the source agencies upon completion of the projects to fully settle the obligations. Moreover, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
observations.managementAction: The LGU have remitted the deficiency to GSIS the remitted contributions. However, the Management will coordinate to GSIS to reconcile and update records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry to correct the misclassification of account amounting to ₱65,100.00 in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information. Moreover, we recommended that Management send notice to the concerned creditors to file their claims by submitting the necessary documents, otherwise, revert to the Government Equity the amount of ₱94,800.00 which remained unclaimed for more than ten (10) years in line with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Management is still in the process of sending notices to the concerned creditors to file their claims by submitting the necessary documents. The unaccounted beginning balance will be reverted back to Government Equity.
observations.reason: Needs further monitoring and evaluation

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant identify the cause(s) of the deficiency and immediately coordinate with the GSIS to reconcile and update agency/employee records. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure full and timely deduction and remittance of GSIS contributions of employees in strict adherence to Section 6 of R.A. No. 8291, also known as the Government Service Insurance System Act of 1997.
observations.managementAction: The LGU have remitted to GSIS the deficiency. However, for the deficiency generated from the EBCS, the Management will still coordinate with GSIS to reconcile its records.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and update its Real Property Tax Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card based on the database and assessment roll of the Municipal Assessor and thereafter forward a copy of certified list of the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to the Municipal Accountant as the basis in setting-up the Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable; Instruct the Municipal Accountant to set-up the Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable at the beginning of the year which shall exclude the advance payments already collected during the previous year; and Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit the master list of RPT delinquencies to the Municipal Accountant and require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to evaluate and analyze the discrepancies between the tax delinquencies of RPT reported in the financial statements as of the year ending December 31, 2023 and the list of tax delinquencies extracted from the electronic Government Application System (e-GAPS). Thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information in view of Section 27 of International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS 1) and Section 111 of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Assessor are in the process of reconciling records. Management will evaluate the balance of the SEF and ensure inclusion of unexpended balances in the SEF budget for the ensuing year.
observations.reason: Requires continuous monitoring and evaluation

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to create an Inventory Committee to spearhead the planning and the conduct of physical count of all PPE of the Municipality and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGU and COA Circular No. 2020-006.

Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General Services Officer to prepare Property Cards and to reconcile balances thereof with that of the accounting records regularly.

We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel to exert effort in retrieving reports or documents to identify the properties recorded under the SEF.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Disposal and Appraisal Committee to fast track the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties to avoid further deterioration and consequent reduction in their value.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the carrying amount of unserviceable properties after the disposal and to recognize the gain or loss arising therefrom.

Henceforth, all properties subject for disposal should be supported with IIRUP and WMR and should be inspected by the head of the agency or his duly authorized representative in the presence of the auditor concerned.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to:

Submit the prior authority from the local sanggunian and the specific sources of funds used for the time deposits to establish whether these are essentially idle funds;

Verify the source and purpose of the funds to ascertain the funds’ actual status and nature so that appropriate accounting entries can be prepared in the books of the agency; and

Re-evaluate the placement of time deposits as investment or terminate and transfer these funds to the appropriate bank account to be made available for projects that could immediately benefit constituents.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make adjusting entries to record the proceeds from sale of bidding documents as Trust Liabilities.

Henceforward, we also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize as revenue any excess amount after the payment of BAC honoraria.

We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure to secure a separate authorization from the local sanggunian in cases of PPAs with lump sum appropriations described in generic terms. Henceforth, the Municipal Development Council ensure that projects incorporated in the AIP are properly planned and provided with sufficient details such as project location aside from the amount.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Solid Waste Management Board to revisit the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management (SWM) Plan and implement the same in accordance with Republic Act No. (RA) 9003.


We also recommended and the Management agreed to enforce the closure of the open dumpsite, develop and create a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site to ensure protection of public health and environment.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the MENRO to submit SWM Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report for CY 2019

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials and employees to submit the feasibility study (FS) and business plan (BP) for the Municipal Inland Resort and Training Center to establish the economic and social gains to be derived there from as a local economic enterprise and as a performance measure in evaluating its sustainability. Otherwise, submit an explanation as to why no FS and BP was conducted prior to establishment of the structures.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the unliquidated fund transfers with the Source Agencies and prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items noted.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  direct the Disposal Committee to follow  the procedures and guidelines on the disposal as well as the forms to be used in reporting thereof.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Engineer to properly plan and monitor all infrastructure projects so as to avoid any delays and incurrence of additional costs.
We further recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2015 PEI amounting to ₱2,262,654.50.
We also recommended that the concerned municipal officials and employees cause the refund of the total amount of ₱293,440.00 representing the traveling expenses incurred on Lakbay Aral.
We also recommended that all Municipal Officials and employees cause the refund of the total amount of ₱1,782,000.00 representing the traveling expenses of the 82 regular personnel of the LGU.
We recommended that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the CY 2014 Productivity Enhancement Incentive at ₱10,000.00 each for the 125 employees.
We recommended that Management cause the refund of the 2013 productivity enhancement incentive at ₱15,000.00 each for the 118 employees and at ₱10,500.00 each for the 8 employees.
We recommended that Management cause the refund of the amount in excess of ₱5,000.00 for each employee granted under E.O. No. 80.

The Local Chief Executive shall ensure the titling of all parcels of land owned by the Municipality and by prioritizing the appropriation of funds for the purpose of establishing ownership thereof and thus avoid future litigation.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to establish the appropriate subsidiary ledgers of the Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education Fund Receivables. Thereafter, evaluate the status of its receivables and if the circumstances warrant its write-off, observe the guidelines and procedures on the dormant receivable accounts prescribed in COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the records affecting the unreconciled balances and improperly recorded expense accounts, provide depreciation expenses thereof, and take up all necessary adjustments in the books in order to establish the fair balance of the PPE in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management observe the rules and regulations on the payment of honoraria to personnel involved in government procurements. In this regard, the required lacking documents for the payment of honoraria amounting to P630,229.59, be submitted for verification. Should the required documents be not submitted or found not in order, the disbursement may be suspended or disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Management shall ensure that all cash advances, except for travels, shall be made by the accountable officers who are properly designated and properly bonded pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002. There should no additional cash advances be granted to those with unliquidated cash advances and that it should be disbursed only for purposes allowed under the said COA Circular. Lastly, the municipal accountant is required to monitor the liquidation of cash advances at the end of the year and ensure that there should be nil amount thereof.

We recommended that Management to monitor the proper and timely implementation of the project and determine the cause of delay in its implementation. The Management should consider the possible imposition of sanctions including payment of liquidated damages or termination of contract considering the considerable period of time delay in the completion of the project.

We recommended that Management stop granting additional honoraria to the BEOs as it constitutes double compensation which is strictly proscribed by law. The Management may consider other forms of support to help and promote the maintenance of peace and order within its locality without violating the rules on the grant of compensation for barangay officials and personnel.

We recommended that Management require officials and employees concerned to adhere strictly to the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 to facilitate monitoring and preparation of reports relative to fuel consumption and to minimize wastage, excessive and unnecessary expenditures. Instruct drivers to accomplish properly the Driver's Trip Tickets and prepare on a monthly basis, the Report of Official Travels.

Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to exert more efforts in securing the available and necessary documents/information pertaining to the Other Receivables account under the Special Education Fund and determine whether these are sufficient to assert validity of claim of LGU Cabatuan, Isabela; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to consider the provision of allowance for impairment pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 and/or request for the write-off of the dormant receivables, if appropriate, to the Commission on Audit in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary disclosures required by the relevant IPSASs and the NGAS Manual for LGUs particularly the assumption and estimates used in recognizing the Impairment Loss – Loans and Receivables account and/or the events and circumstances leading to the impairment loss.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to correct the accounting error to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant secure the available documents, send notice to the concerned creditors to inform them to submit the lacking documents to facilitate the payment of the amount due, otherwise, revert to the Government Equity the unliquidated balance of accounts payable amounting to ₱146,431.61 in line with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445, to come up with a reliable and accurate financial information. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the Ageing Schedule for the Accounts Payable under the Trust Fund to ensure fair and accurate presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the unexpended balances of fund transfers from other LGUs, NGAs and GOCCs aggregating to ₱654,104.09 be reverted back to the source agencies so that it could be used for other beneficial projects pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to correct the accounting error to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

Execute Memoranda of Agreement (MOA) with the concerned schools in relation to the implementation of school projects and activities and define the terms and conditions of the fund transfer, and the responsibilities of both parties; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to henceforth recognize fund transfers to national government agencies as Due to National Government Agencies, if such transfers are with condition, binding agreement, and specific purpose, to provide accurate and more reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary disclosures required by the relevant IPSASs and the NGAS Manual for LGUs particularly on the Government Equity account to ensure the fair presentation of the account in the CY 2024 financial statements.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that grants transferred to Non-Government Organizations (NGOs)/People’s Organization (POs) are properly recorded under the Due from NGO/POs account and that appropriate procedures for liquidation are followed; and
Address the deficiencies noted above by ensuring strict adherence to all the requirements provided for in COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2007, and by ensuring that all necessary documents by the partner NGOs/POs are submitted, verified, and compliant with the prescribed guidelines before the release of any government funds.

Henceforth ensure that the appropriated amounts of the 20% Development Fund are fully utilized in the implementation of the lined-up projects contained in the approved Annual Investment Plan (AIP); and
Direct the Municipal Engineer, in cooperation with the concerned heads of Implementing Departments, to closely monitor the utilization and implementation of priority projects which are essential to the promotion of general welfare of the people and to maximize the usage of the 20% development fund as mandated under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Person to submit the GAD Plan and Budget, along with the Accomplishment Reports and their supporting documents, and strictly observe the period prescribed under Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014, to ensure the timely evaluation by the Audit Team of these reports.

We recommended that Management formulate a comprehensive and well-defined policy and guidelines in the granting of donations and financial assistance-AICS that would ensure equitable allocation of funds among the constituents to enable the LGU to systematically provide financial assistance to more beneficiaries and safeguard the government resources against loss or wastage in line with Section 2 of P.D No. 1445.

Submit the copies of the contracts and its supporting documents and henceforth, ensure that copies of contracts are submitted within the prescribed period of five (5) days from issuance thereof in strict adherence to item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, to avoid the issuance of audit suspension/s on the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents; and
The Municipal Mayor or the Municipal HRMO submit the required certification that the activities/services cannot be provided by regular or permanent personnel of the agency to support the corresponding payments of salaries and wages in compliance with Item 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

1.9. We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to (i) book up the reconciling items totaling ₱6,451,172.20; (ii) prepare the BRSs for all bank accounts to ensure that the cash in bank ledger balance tallied with their corresponding bank balance; and (iii) exert more effort in determining the nature and details of the difference of ₱722,319.22 as well as the long-outstanding bank reconciling items to facilitate immediate adjustments in the books.

2.11. We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct (a) all accountable officers and employees to ensure liquidation of cash advances within the prescribed periods required under COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; (b) City Accountant, in collaboration with the City Legal Officer, to intensify collection measures or institute appropriate remedies that would require accountable officers to settle or liquidate their past due accounts; and (c) require the City Accountant to initiate the procedures for the application of request for write off of dormant accounts, following the guidelines on the proper disposition of dormant accounts under COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023, amending COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

3.14. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the (a) City Treasurer to (i) continue exerting best effort in improving the tax collection efficiency of the City; and (ii) issue warrants of levy on real properties of delinquent taxpayers, as directed under Section 258 of R.A. No. 7160, to avoid possible dismissal from the service, as provided in Section 259 of the same law; and (b) the City Accountant to (i) correct the amount of the set-up of Real Property Tax Receivable and its corresponding Deferred Real Property Tax account by reducing them by ₱222.43; and (ii) coordinate with the City Treasurer in the annual assessment of the impairment of the RPT and SET Receivables; and (iii) record the amount of the resulting impairment, if any.

4.19. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Inventory Committee to (i) conduct the physical count of stocks of all supplies and materials of the City in coordination with the CGSO and all the department heads and offices; (ii) prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories and submit the same to the Auditor; and (iii) reconcile the resultant report with the accounting records; (b) CGSO to strictly comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated inventory records; (c) City Accountant to (i) observe prudence in accounting of inventories; (ii) put in place the appropriate controls on inventory transactions in order to render correct balances of the accounts; and (iii) effect the necessary adjustment/s in the books and SLCs to reconcile the variance of ₱5,090,276.23 on Inventory balance.

5.19. We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the LCE require the (a) Inventory Committee and Acting CGSO Head to (i) conduct a complete physical inventory of all PPEs of the City on or before January 31 of each year, conclude said inventory count for CY 2024 and immediately submit the related reports to the Audit Team; (ii) reconcile the resultant inventory reports with the accounting records; and (iii) indicate the required information in the RPCPPE; (b) Acting CGSO Head to (i) maintain complete PPE records such as PCs for the proper control and monitoring of the agency’s property and equipment; (c) City Accountant to (i) coordinate with the CGSO and Inventory Committee for the reconciliation of respective records and effect the necessary corrections/adjustments, if warranted, in the books of accounts; and (d) City Accountant and Acting CGSO Head to consider the guidelines on one-time cleansing of PPE accounts to render a reliable PPE information.

6.23. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) responsible officers to (i) observe prudence on project management, implementation, and monitoring of the PPAs to facilitate the transfer of completed projects to the appropriate asset accounts for fair presentation of balances in the financial statements; (ii) coordinate properly with concerned offices to ensure accurate reporting of information on PPAs; (iii) impose liquidated damages on erring contractors who incurred negative slippages on construction or material delays in the performance of contractual obligation; (iv) institute appropriate legal remedy against the contractors with unsettled advance payments; (b) City Accountant to (i) reclassify all drainage canals recorded in the current and previous years, if any, from the Water Supply Systems account to the Flood Control Systems account; (ii) reconstruct the records of account with incomplete information and work closely with responsible officers on the accurate reporting and disclosure of the Advance Payments and CIP accounts, particularly the unsubstantiated carried-over balance of ₱4,403,582.21; and (iii) coordinate with the CPD Officer for the reconciliation of their year-end records for the accurate presentation of the amounts of CIP in the financial statements and the Infrastructure Accomplishment Report.

7.22. We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the City Accountant to (a) notify the payees of the unsettled DSWD trust fund totaling ₱72,050.00 to fully liquidate the amount received, and if efforts would be futile, revert back the fund to the Source Agency; (b) immediately revert the unspent ₱3,817,892.07 LGSF-OFA to the Bureau of Treasury; and (c) properly dispose the dormant accounts worth ₱1,234,857.73 in accordance with the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2023-008. We likewise recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to reclassify the fund transfer from PCSO worth ₱661,182.48 from Due to NGAs to Due to GOCCs.

8.26. We recommended that Management: (a) recognize and make necessary adjustments in the Employee Benefits account, update/reconcile employee leave credits and perform other relevant activities within three years from the effectivity of COA Circular No. 2024-009; and (b) submit the lacking documents listed in Appendices A, A-1 and A-2 and ensure that the disbursement vouchers are supported with complete documentation in compliance with Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

9.18. We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the: (a) City Treasurer, CBLIPO, and Sangguniang Panlungsod to (i) coordinate for the updating of the Revenue Code of Cabanatuan City, containing adjustments in the rates of taxes to optimize its revenue generation for the benefit of its constituents; and (ii) ensure that the amended Revenue Code is responsive to the needs of the City and complies with the Local Government Code of 1991; (b) City Treasurer to (i) use available records in validating the declared sales of taxpayers in securing business permits with the City; and (ii) secure from the OCA any report that can be useful in the assessment of business taxes for business entities that have transaction with the City; (c) CBLIPO to be more strict in the implementation and enforcement of the City Revenue Code; and (d) City Treasurer and CBLIPO to verify, assess, and validate the records of business establishments and demand the payment of the computed under collection of business taxes amounting to ₱1,896,577.70.
10.7. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) through the City Accountant ensure full compliance with the disclosure requirements provided under IPSAS 3.

11.39. We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE): (a) ensure the full implementation of all Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) and the optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund during the budget year; (b) instruct the (i) City Engineer to conduct thorough feasibility or preliminary engineering studies and thresh out imminent or possible problems in the implementation of infrastructure projects that are to be included in the 20% Development Fund priority list; and (ii) the City Engineer, City Planning and Development Officer and other concerned officials to thoroughly study all feasible solutions in order to swiftly and successfully complete the ongoing and suspended projects for the immediate benefit of the constituents; (c) require the concerned officers to be prudent in overseeing the timely implementation of the PPAs under their respective responsibilities, impose appropriate sanctions upon the contractors who incur breach of contract and submit copies of Work Suspension Orders; (d) direct the City Accountant to (i) submit immediately to the Auditor the disbursement vouchers listed in Appendix C-1 to facilitate the audit of the transactions pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and (ii) review and complete the requisite documentation of procurements; and (e) require the BAC, TWG and City Accountant to submit the lacking documents detailed in Appendix C-2 to facilitate the determination of the validity, completeness and correctness of the expenses, pursuant to Section 4, paragraph 6 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

11.40. We further reiterated our recommendation that the LCE impose liquidated damages computed at ₱9,354,076.47 against the contractors for failing to commence and finish the construction of the projects including Calle Vicente Flyover and if deemed appropriate, rescind the contract pursuant to Section 68 of RA No. 9184.

12.9. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the CSWD Officer continue complying with the Magna Carta of Women, PCW-DILG-DBM- NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01, and PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01 on gender mainstreaming and delivering more programs that are gender-responsive to all the City’s constituents.

13.15. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the CDRRM Officer to: (a) ensure the optimal utilization of the Mitigation Fund by realigning current year savings and prior years’ savings in the Special Trust Fund to fully implement the planned priority PPAs for disaster risk reduction and climate change adaptation; and (b) evaluate the necessity of appropriating for PPAs by coordination during the budgeting phase with other City government department heads who could be probably implementing the same PPAs to avoid redundancy of PPAs.

14.22. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) (a) consider the revision of the policy on the recruitment and employment of Job Order workers to conform with the applicable provisions of the appurtenant rules and regulations; (b) increase the plantilla positions and hire regular workers instead of heavily relying on JO workers in the performance of routine government works; (c) raise the compensation of current JO workers to the salaries of comparable government positions as required by Section 9 of COA-DBM JC. No. 2, s. 2020 dated October 20, 2020; and (d) instruct the HRMO and the OCA to ensure that the Accomplishment Reports submitted by the JO workers contain the expected output and the corresponding actual and quantified accomplishments.

15.12. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) continue addressing the root cause/s of insurgency and other armed conflicts by delivering projects under the LGSF-SBDP for the benefit of the City’s constituents. We likewise recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to (a) prepare the quarterly reports and status of program/project implementation on fund utilization; (b) coordinate with the City Engineer and the City Planning and Development Officer for the mandatory posting of the said reports in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in the City and in the City’s website; and (c) send written notice of the posting of program implementation and fund utilization to the oversight bodies, as prescribed in Section 5.4 of DBM LBC No. 154.

16.22. We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct the CE and the CPDO to enhance their compliance with the publicity and notification of City’s PPAs by: (a) preparing and submitting, at the beginning of the year, the list of all on-going government PPAs and those to be implemented during the year; (b) provide proof of installation of signboards on the 12 on-going projects, otherwise cause the refund of the cost of tarpaulin signboards as indicated in the program of works; (c) closely monitor the installation of signboards/tarpaulins by the contractor/supplier and cause the immediate rectification had there been incomplete details or erroneous inscription thereon; (d) notify the Audit Team within 10 days that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted in the project site; and (e) employ close supervision on the implementation of PPAs, and maintain a reliable mechanism on maintaining and updating the monthly reports on PPAs’ accomplishments.

17.11. We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the 36 un-transmitted DVs and their supporting documents to the Audit Team. Continuous failure to comply with this recommendation may result to the suspension of payment of salaries of the officials responsible, as well as of the cost of unsubmitted DVs.

18.7. We recommended that the CGSO Head submit copies of Contracts and POs together with their supporting documents within five days from date of execution in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 for the timely conduct of contract review, inspection and other necessary audit procedures by the Audit Team.

2.6 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) advise the Municipal Accountant (MA) and Municipal Treasurer (MT) to continue its faithful compliance with the withholding and full remittance of the taxes withheld within the prescribed period.

3.6 We recommended that the LCE advise the MAO and Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO) to consistently maintain their good practice of religiously observing the prescribed deadlines on the withholding of premiums/deductions and the respective remittance thereof to the GSIS in pursuance of the GSIS Act of 1997.

4.6 We recommended that the Management continue to adopt the good practice of promptly remitting premiums, loan amortizations, and other contributions to the HDMF in compliance with the provisions of R.A. No. 9679.
5.4 We recommended that the LCE require the MAO and the MTO continue to be compliant with regulations on withholding and remittance of monthly PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period.

6.17 We commended the Management for its compliance with regulations governing the granting, utilization, and recording of CAs issued to SDOs. However, we still recommended providing stringent measures in the liquidations of the CAs and granting of additional CAs only upon the liquidation of previous related CAs. Moreover, we also recommended designating other SDOs to accommodate some of the CAs and prevent overlapping ones.

We recommended that the LCE:
a) direct the ME, as the Building Official, to inform all applicants for building permits of their post-construction responsibilities, including the
mandatory filing of a sworn declaration of the true value of the structure
upon completion; and
b) require the OMA to declare all identified undeclared properties in the
name of the defaulting owner, if known, or under an “unknown owner”
classification, as the case may be, and assess them for taxation in
accordance with existing laws, rules, and regulations.

8.18 We recommended that the Management consider the following remedial
measures:
a) prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts;
b) direct the MT/GSO to maintain complete, detailed PCs to ensure that
property records are properly reconciled with the accounting records; and
c) consider hiring additional manpower under GSO to facilitate the proper
safekeeping and maintenance of records of all PPE accounts.

9.22 We recommended that the LCE:
a) instruct the MA to:
i. draw the following accounting entries to correct the misstatements in
Leave Benefits Payable and Government Equity accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Leave Benefits Payable ₱50,507.73
Government Equity ₱50,507.73
ii. reclassify the non-current portion of the Leave Benefits Payable for
the proper presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial
Position;
b) direct the HRMO to:
i. submit the six unsubmitted leave cards to the AT for further
evaluation;
ii. initiate and prioritize the reconciliation and correction of balances in
the leave cards; and
iii. revisit its process for updating and maintaining leave cards.
Specifically, the revised process should include regularly updating
leave cards and other internal control measures to provide timely and
accurate updates;
c) enjoin the MA and the HRMO to institute a periodic audit of all employees’ accumulated leaves before approval and payment of their respective monetized leave credits and terminal leave benefits; and
d) set aside additional reserve funds for the accumulated compensated leave
credits as of year-end; henceforth, ensure the adequacy of the funds
provided for the leave credits earned thereafter by including the said
benefits in the annual budget.

10.17 We recommended that the LCE require the MA to sort out the various funds
and use them for the furtherance of the improvement of the LGU as well as use or
return the funds in consonance with COA Circular No. 2020-04 dated January 20,
2021 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994, whichever
warrants and ensure that all funds received from SAs are supported with a duly
signed MOA/TA to clearly define the specific guidelines and procedures in the
implementation of individual projects, including the responsibilities of the
Municipality, thereby ensuring timely accomplishment thereof for the benefit of the
intended beneficiaries.

11.26 We recommended that the LCE:
a) instruct the MA to correct the unadjusted disbursement in the Trust
Liability-DRRM account in the TF books amounting to ₱15,450.00 and
transfer the savings to the said account amounting to ₱93,984.25; and
b) direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) include in the preparation of the
LDRRMFIP/AIP the list of PPAs to be undertaken out of the unexpended
balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize the use of the STF; and
(ii) strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs
to achieve the goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits.

12.15 We recommended that the LCE instruct the LSB to:
a) ensure that the date of transmittal of the copy of the SEF Budget to the
concerned departments is indicated in the receiving copy on file; and
b) submit the quarterly and annual reports using the SEF Budget
Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) on SEF utilization, as prepared by
the MA, to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, with a copy
furnished to the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the
DBM and the DILG.

13.30 We recommended that the LCE instruct the:
a) Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the ME together
with the Local Development Council and end-users to (i) ensure that the
identified PPAs under 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-
implementation-ready; and (ii) conduct regular monitoring of the status of the project to
ensure the full implementation of all planned PPAs;
b) BAC to require the winning contractors to (i) submit DOLE-approved
CSHP; and (ii) post-performance security with validity until the issuance
of the Certificate of Final Acceptance and warranty security, which runs
for a period of one year from the date of final acceptance of the project;
c) ME to monitor the proper implementation of CSHP of the contractors,
particularly the provision of personal protective equipment to construction
workers, to ensure its compliance with the requirements of the DOLE; and
d) all winning contractors to submit their respective CSHPs to DOLE for
approval before the implementation of each infrastructure project.

We also recommended that Management consider hiring additional engineers
or technical personnel to help with the preparation of DED and PoWs, if necessary,
to avoid unjustifiable delays in the implementation of development projects.

14.17 We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
established headcount requirements for casual and JO personnel, budget
appropriation for PS within limitation set by R.A. No. 7160, and for maintaining
complete documentary requirements for claims of salaries and wages of casual and
JO personnel. However, we recommended that Management:
a) re-evaluate the current personnel complement to fill-up vacancies; and
b) establish and implement a systematic assessment process to evaluate the
qualifications, competencies and performance of JO and casual personnel
to ensure the Municipality maintains a competent, qualified and effective
workforce.

15.9 We recommended that the LCE:
a) instruct the LGU GFPS to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs to
ensure that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals
and objectives; and
b) instruct the GAD Focal Person to submit the documentary requirements
supporting the GAD AR.

16.4 We recommended that Management enforce immediate compliance on the
audit disallowances as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of 2009 RRSA.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the following actions:
Municipal Accountant to:
Reclassify all completed projects from the CIP account to appropriate asset accounts and take up the corresponding depreciation expenses of the completed projects from the time these were completed;
Derecognize the  amount of ₱94,619.00 from the asset account and recognize the corresponding expense accordingly; and
Ensure the proper recording of the infrastructure assets, whether in the process of construction or already completed, and the related depreciation
Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant after project validation/ inspection and periodically reconcile project status with the accounting records so that the proper adjustments may be made to the CIP accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Devise a plan and take necessary steps to determine the correct balances of PPE accounts that have been recorded without depreciation, ensuring that appropriate journal entries are made to recognize corresponding depreciation and accumulated depreciation and to adjust the Government Equity account;
Transfer the assets to the General Fund after liquidating the fund transfers and provide the necessary depreciation to the affected PPE items to reflect their accurate valuation and correct their carrying amounts; and
Prepare the required entries to derecognize the disposed construction and heavy equipment and correct the Due to LGUs balance, ensuring that PPE and related accounts are properly reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to:
1. Follow the Perpetual Inventory Method in the accounting of inventories in compliance with Section 114 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs Volume I; and
Record stock issuances only upon receipt of the SSMI with the original copy of the Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS).
b. creation of General Services Office, subject to Personal Services Limitation, to:
Formulate, plan, strategies and timeliness in the conduct of semestral physical inventories, reconcile the balances, prepare RPCI and submit the results to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year, for the first and second semesters, respectively;
Maintain stock cards for supplies to track their receipt and disposal and reconcile them with the accounting unit's ledger cards;
Consolidate weekly the RIS for which supplies and materials were issued using the SSMI and submit it immediately, along with the RIS, to the Municipal Accountant for recording of the cost of the supplies and materials issued.
Engineering Office to prepare the list of completed projects and submit the same to the Accounting Office for reconciliation and reclassification of the Construction Materials account; and
Municipal Accountant and the GSO/Municipal Treasurer to maintain the required forms, records, and periodic reports for the recognition of expense portion of the inventories and to reconcile the same.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the:
Municipal Treasurer to provide to the Municipal Accountant a Schedule of Unreleased Checks at year end; and
b.    Municipal Accountant to ensure that only valid issued and released checks are reported as reconciling items in the Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the provincial and barangay shares are included when setting up the RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Record the advance payments on real properties as credit to Deferred RPT or Deferred SET, at gross amount;
Record the discounts on advance payments as a debit to Discount on Advance Payments in the books; and
Recognize as income the advance payments received when related paid taxes become due.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Engineer to ensure that any changes in the original contract must be supported with an approved variation order, accompanied by other relevant documents, pursuant to Annex E of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and the Municipality agreed to refrain from charging non-priority items from the 5% LDRRMF, and formulate/implement DRRM programs, projects, and activities that are congruent with the thematic areas of disaster reduction ad management to strengthen and enhance the Municipality’s disaster capabilities at all levels.

We recommended and the Management agreed to expedite the review and approval of the required 5-year plan for the enactment of an ordinance authorizing the establishment of the Municipal Inland Resort and Training Center as its Local Economic Enterprise to ensure the legality and propriety of its existence in accordance with Section 4.8 of the Manual.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to establish the Municipality’s GAD M&E Team and formalize the monitoring and evaluation of its GAD-related activities through the issuance of an appropriate Executive Order. The GAD M&E system should track PPA progress, record challenges and solutions, and regularly report on the Municipality’s GAD activities.

to record the understatement of the Land account in the books
of accounts in the total amount of ₱230,564,911.00, the
overstatement of the Advances to Contractors account by
₱8,000,000.00 and the understatement of the Accounts payable

1.10.2 The City Accountant and the Acting City Assessor continually
conduct reconciliation of records and retrieve pertinent
documents relating to the unrecorded and unreconciled
parcels of land to facilitate recording of said transactions.

to reclassify the completed projects to their appropriate
Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) accounts in the amount
of ₱6,074,709.01 and to recognize the related accounts

PPE accounts, in accordance with International Public Sector
Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 17 and COA Circular
No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.

5.17.2 The City Engineer fast-track the preparation of the

salaries of those presently employed personnel and elected

No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

7.23.2 The Head of Agency make representations with COA Central

Accountant initiate the request for authority to write off the dormant
and untraceable receivables account in accordance with COA Circular
No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

submission of liquidation documents as prescribed under COA

9.13.2 The City Accountant standardize the issuance of demand

Accountant identify the causes of discrepancies in the corresponding
subsidiary ledgers and immediately effect proper and verifiable
adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the correct account
balances of the reciprocal accounts in all funds maintained by the
City.

11.12 We recommended and the Management agreed that the Accountable
Officer, in this case, the City Agriculturist, submit the required
documents to facilitate her application for relief from accountability in
compliance with COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992 and
COA Memorandum No. 92-751 dated February 24, 1992. Afterwards,
the City Accountant make the necessary adjusting entries to
derecognize the said Breeding Stocks account in the amount of
₱69,000.00 and recognize the pertinent expense or receivable account
depending on the result of the evaluation of the said request for relief.

Services Office immediately act on the disposal of all unserviceable
PPE items in accordance with Section 79 of PD 1445 and COA-DBM
Joint Circular No. 2024-1 dated January 30, 2024 to avoid further
deterioration and losses of the said unserviceable PPEs and fast-track
the application of the one-time cleansing of its PPEs in accordance
with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

13.16 We recommended that the City, through the Acting City Human
Resource Management Officer, assess the current human resource
management practices and capabilities of the City and compare the
same to desired outcomes as prescribed under CSC Memorandum
Circular No. 3, series of 2012 dated February 16, 2012 and other human
resource management best practices, and develop strategies to
address the identified gaps, particularly on position vacancies,
succession planning, and hiring of job orders for regular tasks.

and Development Officer (CPDO), in coordination with the City
Engineering Office and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC),
closely monitor the implementation of planned development projects
of the 20% Development Fund and adopt methods to ensure timely
conduct of the procurement process in order to optimally utilize the
fund and eventually help the City in timely attaining its desired goals
and objectives of the said Fund.

of the delivered units of dump trucks with a general vehicle
weight of 12,000 kilograms and dump trucks with a payload
of 12,000 kilograms in order to validate that the deficiency
noted was only a clerical error and that there was no loss to
the government.

15.19.2 The Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical Working
Group observe proper review process in all phases of the
procurement process to ensure compliance with the
requirements, terms, conditions, and specifications
stipulated in the Bidding Documents, as required under
Sections 18, 20, and 22 of Republic Act 9184 and its Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR).

immediately submit the lacking disbursement vouchers and
their supporting documents, in accordance with Section 100
of PD 1445 and Section 7.2.1.a of COA Circular 2009-006
dated September 15, 2009.

16.14.2 The Management consider increasing the number of
personnel in the City Accounting Office to address
manpower constraints and facilitate the timely submission of
transaction documents to COA.

observations.managementAction:
The MT minimized (a)(i) instances of not
depositing the collections intact daily.
Counter-checking was accurate; and entries recorded in the
cashbook to minimize the possible mis-recording.
Instances of collections for emergency purchases, vales, and the
like have been minimized.
Regular reconciliation between cashbook entries and accounting
ledgers was conducted.

The total recorded CIP account as of year-end was fairly stated
due to the proper reclassification of various completed projects to their
proper PPE accounts and accurate recording of work
accomplishments for various infrastructure projects.
The MA regularly draw JEVs to ensure the timely recognition of CIP
to fairly present the related asset accounts in the monthly FS of the
Municipality.
Likewise, the MEO has been advised on the prompt submission of
monthly monitoring report/Statement of Work Accomplishments (SWA)
regarding the status/progress of the construction of all ongoing
projects to facilitate the timely recording of CIP in the books.
The Municipal Engineer (ME) regularly notifies the MAO on the status
of all ongoing projects.

Verification of the details of the (a) Due to NGA account revealed
that unexpended balances of completed projects totaling ₱299,444.04
remained in the books of accounts subject to remittance to the Bureau
of the Treasury (BTr).
The MAO and MTO have initiated the submission of unsubmitted
accounts from prior periods. An appropriate JEV was drawn to properly
classify the cash prizes received from various agencies totaling
₱19,191.00.
Long outstanding fire code fees amounting to ₱5,990.00 remained in the
books and pending remittance to the BTr.

Delays in the completion of the work of the contractor are still
noted.
Verification of the submitted disbursement vouchers (DVs) and
supporting documents on payments of infrastructure contracts revealed
that the warranty security posted by the winning bidders for three
completed infrastructure projects during the year was insufficient in
terms of validity period.
The Implemented the MAO to scrutinize the validity period of the
performance and warranty security before payment.
The Bids and Awards Committee was instructed to require the winning
contractor to post-performance security with validity until the issuance of
the Certificate of Final Acceptance and warranty security which runs for
a period of one year from the date of final acceptance of the project.
The CSHP were found attached for some projects but were not submitted
to DOLE for approval in some projects.
observations.reason:
The effectivity period of the performance security for three projects did
not cover the whole duration of the defects liability period of one year.
Some CSHP were attached, but were not submitted to DOLE for
approval.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the required adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

Moreover, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to henceforward record the purchases of construction materials for development projects by administration as inventory and the consumption thereof in the Construction in Progress account, as well as, to recognize in the appropriate PPE accounts upon completion.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the discrepancy of ₱(1.478) million in the cashbook of Cash in Bank- Local Currency, GF accounts.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to regularly reconcile their books to ensure that any possible errors are immediately detected and rectified.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare correcting journal entries to reclassify the advance payments made to contractors to Advances to Contractors account instead of Other Receivables, and henceforth, all other advance payments to be made to contractors.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to coordinate with all officials concerned before approving variation orders ensuring that any changes to the contract of projects/programs/activities are sufficiently supported with appropriation.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to properly monitor the implementation of the projects/programs/activities and to coordinate with other offices/departments concerned. Effective monitoring and feedback system in the implementation of projects ensure that Management and officials concerned are updated on the status and that corrective measures can be immediately addressed on problems encountered.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to collect the liquidated damages from the contractor/supplier totaling ₱64,419.50.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive and other officials concerned agreed to monitor the timely delivery of procured goods in accordance with the conditions stipulated in the contract, otherwise, liquidated damages should be imposed.

Henceforth, we further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Engineer, Municipal Accountant and all other personnel concerned to look into the completeness and accuracy of data in all the pertinent documents before payment is made.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the LDRRMO and the Bids and Award Committee that all disaster risk management-related trainings must be done through proper procurement process in accordance with RA No. 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR.

Henceforward, we also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel that training fees should be paid by check directly to the suppliers and withhold the required taxes from payments thereof. Likewise, make sure that the transactions are duly supported with complete documentation before processing the claims.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to diligently review the eligibility of the suppliers/contractors before the award of the contract to ensure that the latter is legally, technically, and financially capable of delivering the required goods or services pursuant to the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly adhere with the guidelines stipulated in DBM BC No. 2004-5A to ensure that honoraria are paid only for successfully completed procurement projects.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel to refrain from using the Petty Cash Fund to pay recurring and regular operating expenses and to strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 2012-001 in the disbursements and replenishment of PCF.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to adequately provide a concise narrative on the amount and nature of restricted cash balances under Note 4 - Cash and Cash Equivalents to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant vouched to implement this recommendation.
observations.reason: As verified by the Audit Team, concise narrative on the amount and nature of restricted cash balances were not provided in the Notes to Financial Statements of the Municipality. Reiterated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended Management to reassess feasibility of the project taking into consideration the present condition of the eleven (11) OTOP mobile stores through ocular inspection. We reiterated that the City Mayor require the (a) accountable officers to improve further their compliance on the reporting/liquidation of cash advances; and (b) City Accountant, in collaboration with the City Legal Officer, to intensify collection measures or institute appropriate remedy that would require accountable officers to settle or liquidate their past due accounts; and (c) if conditions and circumstances on writing off the accounts would be present, direct the City Accountant and the Legal officer to initiate the procedures for the application of the request under COA Circular 2016-005.

We reiterated our recommendations  that Local Chief Executive  direct the  (a)  Inventory Committee to  (i) expedite the completion of the  physical count of stocks of all supplies and materials of the City  in coordination with the CGSO and all the department heads and offices; (ii) reconcile the resultant report with the accounting records; (b) CGSO to  strictly comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated inventory records; (c) City Accountant to (i) observe the prudence in accounting of inventory; (ii) put in place the appropriate controls on inventory transactions in order to render correct balances of the accounts; and (iii) provide competent evidence to document the completeness of the inventory transactions.

We reiterated our recommendation  that the Local Chief Executive direct the: (a) Inventory Committee to (i) expedite the completion of the physical inventory of all PPE of the City in coordination with City General Services Office (CGSO) and all the department heads and offices; (ii) reconcile the resultant report with the accounting records; (iii) use the prescribed format of RPCPPE and indicate the required information therein; (b) CGSO to  strictly comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated property records; (c) City Accountant to (i) coordinate with the CGSO and Inventory Committee for the reconciliation of respective records and effect the necessary corrections/adjustments, if warranted, in the books of accounts; and (ii) adjust the books for the misclassified accounts as follows:

Dr. Power Supply Systems ₱5,059,972.71

Dr. Accumulated Depreciation-Other Machineries and Equipment 774,076.99

Cr. Other Machineries and Equipment 5,049,972.71 

Cr. Accumulated Depreciation-Power Supply Systems 774,076.99

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) City Treasurer to discontinue the imposition upon taxpayers of unauthorized fees and charges unless proven that the act of collection is supported by an ordinance enacted by the Sanggunian Panlungsod; and (b) City Accountant to revert the accumulated balance of ₱64,589,037.82 to the General Fund for inclusion in the annual budget/supplemental budget for proper appropriation. We also recommended that responsible offices include in their budget the regular expenditure which they use to charge in the aforementioned Trust Fund accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation  that the City Mayor  ensure the full or optimum utilization of the 20% Development during the budget year; (a) instruct the City Engineer to (i) conduct a thorough feasibility or preliminary engineering study and thresh out imminent or possible problems in the implementation of infrastructure projects that are to be included in the 20% Development Fund priority list; (ii) require the concerned officers to be prudent in overseeing the timely implementation of PPAs under their respective responsibility; (iii) impose appropriate sanctions upon the contractors who incur breach of the contract or agreement; and (iv) order the City Accountant to submit immediately to the Auditor the disbursement vouchers that are due for transmittal.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the City Mayor should  instruct the City Accountant to (a) prepare the liquidation reports for the NHA housing project Phase I, and submit the necessary disbursement reports and documents to COA Office for the audit; (b) notify the payees of the DSWD trust fund, and if efforts would be futile, revert back the fund to the sponsoring agency; (c) immediately revert the unspent ₱3,817,892.07 LGSF-OFA to the Bureau of Treasury; (d) close to  Government Equity account the  misclassified funds of ₱301,776.77; and (e) continue to produce efforts on record retrieval to settle the dormancy of the non-moving Due to NGA accounts and to substantiate the information in the financial reports.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) responsible officers to (i) observe prudence on project management, implementation, and monitoring of the PPAs; (ii) coordinate properly with concerned offices to ensure accurate reporting of information on PPAs; (iii) impose liquidated damages on erring contractors who incurred negative slippages on construction or material delays in the performance of contractual obligation; (iv) institute appropriate legal remedy against the contractors with unsettled advance payments; (b) City Engineer to present to the Audit Team the approved notices appurtenant to projects’ work suspension; and (c) City Accountant to reconstruct the records of account with incomplete information and work closely with responsible officers on the accurate reporting and disclosure of the Advance Payments and CIP accounts.

We recommended that the Local School Board ensure the optimum utilization of the SEF during the budget year and coordinate with the City Engineer, City Planning and Development Office and the BAC Secretariat for the proper planning, timely implementation and monitoring of the PPAs.

We also reiterate our recommendation that LSB be mindful of the instructions of the above JC and instruct the City Accountant to ensure that all disbursements are supported with a declaration, specifically stating that the expenditure of SEF is net of the budgetary provision for DepEd.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City Mayor require the   City Accountant to (a) cause the immediate submission of un-transmitted CYs 2021 and 2022 accounts, records and reports  to the Audit Team; and (b) exert extra efforts and plan out efficient ways to reduce, if not completely eliminate, the continuous incurrence of delays in the submission of accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the DC to (1) expedite the timely disposal of unserviceable properties to avoid further deterioration but to derive better proceeds from sale; and (2) City Accountant to properly disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the information of assets for disposal and those unserviceable assets contained in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP).

No. 7160; and
