observations.recommendation:
1.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to:
a) Take up the necessary adjusting/correcting journal entries in the books of
accounts of the municipality to fairly present the advance payments of RPT
and SET collected in CY 2023, to wit:
RPT Revenue 1,3301941.73
SET Revenue 1,330,941.73
Discount on Advance Payments 532,376.57
Deferred RPT 1,330,941.73
Deferred SET 1,330,941.73
Discount on RPT 266,188.29
Discount on SET 266,188.28
b) Adhere to the correct accounting procedures for RPT and SET collections
to accurately reflect the amounts of RPT and SET income, receivables and
other affected accounts in the financial statements.

2.25. We recommended that the LCE direct the:
a) GSO-designate and Municipal Accountant to have a close coordination
between the Property and Accounting Division for the regular
reconciliation of PPE records and maintain property cards;
b) Municipal Accountant to compute and consistently provide the correct
depreciation for the road network system of which no residual value shall
be recognized and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry as
presented below:
Depreciation Expense 584,741.35
Prior Period Adjustment 1,557,943.53
Accumulated Depreciation Expense 2,142,684.88
c) Municipal Accountant to restate the CY 2023 consolidated financial
statements by reflecting the correct depreciation expenses for CYs 2020 to
2023 in all the affected components of the financial statements.

2.26. We further recommended that the LCE:
a) Create an Inventory Committee, who are temporarily relieve of all their
regular duties, to prepare PIP containing the specific assignments/duties of
the committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the
dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start to finish;
b) Submit the approved PIP to the COA Audit Team at least ten calendar
days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; and
c) Enjoin the Inventory Committee, GSO-designate and Municipal
Accountant to undertake the procedural for the one-time cleansing of PPE
to come up with more reliable account balances to be presented in the
financial statements.

3.7. We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to make the
necessary narrative disclosures as to the amount and nature of the restricted
cash balances under Note 4 – Cash and Cash Equivalents in the financial
statements to ensure compliance to regulatory and legal requirements, and
proper and efficient fund allocation.

4.13. We reiterated our recommendations that the LCE direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a) Determine and adjust the unreconciled reciprocal transactions for CY
2023 totaling to at least ₱2,107,274.90 supported by duly prepared CY 2023
general ledgers and journal entry vouchers for its corresponding
adjustments;
b) Identify the nature of the transaction for the adjustment made to the CY
2023 intra-agency payable totaling ₱2,413,097.37 and submit the
supporting documents for the corresponding journal entry;
c) Determine whether the previous Municipal Accountant recognized the
unrecorded receivable/payable accounts pertaining to the CY 2021 and CY
2020 misstatements with the following journal entries:
Due from Other Funds 4,450.70
Interest Income 4,450.70
Due to BIR 178,157.90
Due to Other Funds 178,157.90
If the same was not yet adjusted, recognize the aforementioned
adjustments to correct the unreconciled intra-agency transactions; and
d) Trace the discrepancies between the balances of the accounts Due from
Other Funds and Due to Other Funds amounting to ₱5,561,271.48, and
effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts through the
preparation of journal entry voucher (JEV) duly supported with documents,
explanations and/or computations in order to reflect the correct balances of
accounts in the financial statements. Henceforth, ensure that reciprocal
accounts have the same balances all the time.

5.14. We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) Recognize allowance for impairment of receivables in accordance with
IPSAS, pending the request for write-off of the dormant receivables, to
present fairly the financial statements; and
b) In coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal
Agriculturist, prepare all the necessary documents needed in support for
the request for write-off such as the following, as applicable:
● Death certificate issued by Philippine Statistics Authority;
● Proof of insolvency;
● Certificate of no residence in the barangay of the municipality/city of
last known address;
● Proof of exhaustion of all remedies to collect the Due from NGO/POs
receivables such as copies of served or returned demand letters;
● Certification by the responsible officials of the entity to the effect that
there are no records/documents available to validate claim; and
● Other justifications, like in the case of request for write-off due to loss
of documents, the circumstances of the loss should be stated in the
letter-request.
c) Thereafter, we recommend that the LCE request for authority to write-off
dormant receivable accounts.

6.7. We recommended that the LCE direct the:
a) Municipal Assessor to resume providing the list of assessed real properties
to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office;
b) Municipal Treasurer to use the list provided by the Municipal Assessor in
updating the RPTAR/TIC which will be used as the basis in the
preparation of the revised list of RPT receivables, and to submit certified
list of taxpayers’ names, and the amount due and collectible for each year
to the Municipal Accountant; and
c) Municipal Accountant to draw a JEV to set-up an accurate RPT
Receivable and SET Receivable accounts to fairly present the actual
financial position.

7.9. We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to exert effort
to prepare and submit the BRS and maintain SL for all bank accounts. In
addition, the Municipal Accountant to regularly reconcile his records with the
balances in the cashbook and adjust any discrepancies, if any.

8.9. We therefore recommended that the LCE convene the department heads to
determine the appropriate manner of liquidation of the long outstanding
payables under the Due to NGAs account amounting to ₱193,000.00 and
thereafter make representations to the concerned NGAs for assistance in the
liquidation of the said funds.

8.10. We further recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to
continue looking into previous years’ files to determine the specific donor
agencies of the undetermined balances from prior years’ amounting to
₱343,945.08 and if warranted, liquidate the payables immediately to have a fair
presentation of financial accounts.

9.21 We recommended that the LCE require the:
a) Municipal Accountant together with the BAC to ensure that all pertinent
documents are attached in the DVs to substantiate the legality, validity and
propriety of the said transactions; and
b) Municipal Accountant to exercise utmost prudence and diligence in his
duties and responsibilities in verifying the completeness of the documentary
requirements for all the transactions before payments are made.

10.12 We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE direct the BAC to:
a) Judiciously conduct post-qualification by verifying, validating, and
ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder
with the Lowest Calculated Bid; and
b) Ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contain an in-depth result on
verifying technical requirements.

11.6 We recommended that Management ensure that all fuel issuance slips are
signed by the authorized issuing official of the municipality and to properly
account and control gasoline, oil and lubricant consumption. We further
recommended that Management ensure the completeness of information in the
Driver’s Trip Ticket and fuel issuance slip by indicating all the information
required in the forms to support the reasonableness of fuel purchased and
consumed for official travels.

12.9 We recommended that the LCE direct the GFPS Technical Working Group
to:
a) Leverage the available tools to strengthen gender analysis and gender
assessment to facilitate effective identification of PPAs to be included
under the GAD fund;
b) Increase the number of organization-focused PPAs intended to capacitate the
GFPS and the rest of the implementing offices on how to undertake
gender analysis in the GPB and implement the same; and
c) Institutionalize the LGU GAD database so that gender issues in the
municipality can be more readily determined and addressed.

We recommended that Management:
initiate an inquiry with the identified account with regard to the balance of the real property tax receivable in the books and request a certification thereof duly signed by the Agency Accountant and the Head of the Agency;
instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to conduct a thorough assessment, evaluation and investigation of the outstanding accounts and record adjustments thereof, if any; and
direct the Municipal Accountant to establish the real property tax receivable account at the beginning of the year and deduct the corresponding collection during the year to arrive at the net real property tax receivable at the of the end year, thus, the real property tax receivable in the next financial reporting will have a new balance and likely to move every year going forward.

We reiterated our previous recommendations since Management unacted the same, to wit:
create a committee that will rigorously pursue the collection of prior year receivables from the identified accountable officers/debtors. Relative to this, Management shall exhaust all possible efforts to collect the receivables such as, but not limited to instituting legal actions against delinquent borrowers, as applicable; and
direct the Municipal Accountant to review the status of the receivables and observe the guidelines and procedures on the write-off of Dormant Receivable accounts as prescribed in COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management submit the documentary requirements to support the grant of BAC Honoraria as required under COA Circular 2012-001.
Likewise, the concerned BAC Members, TWG and BAC Secretariat are required to immediately refund in full the total amount of P503,134.44 based on the above computation as prescribed under BIR Revenue Regulations No. 11-2018 through the issuance of Notice of Disallowance No. 2024-001 dated January 30, 2024.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Local Sanggunian shall revisit the locally-enacted resolutions if the same are still relevant/reasonable nowadays considering the cheaper prevailing rates offered by the mobile networks. Otherwise, the enjoined individuals should decrease the communication allowance to a more conscionable rate. A detailed report must also be submitted to justify the excess, indicating therein the breakdown of expenses covered by the monthly communication allowance given.
Moreover, refrain the giving of communication allowance for those officials and employees which are not covered with the Ordinance and/or not authorized to receive the same and ask the concerned officials to refund the excess claim made as indicated above.
Lastly, the Local Sanggunian should issue a resolution pertaining to the grant of communication allowance indicating therein the reasons and/or justifications along with the list of LGU officials and employees that are entitled to receive the same instead of a mere Municipal Ordinance that only lodged a lump sum figures of allotted communication expense per office.

We recommended that the Management use the maximum monthly compensation of P5,000.00 per month as basis in computing the employer share as provided in RA 9679 or a maximum of P100.00 per month regardless of the amount that the employee contributes for the personal share, in conformity to the IRR of RA 9679.

We recommended that Management ensure that all procurements of the Municipality be paid directly to the suppliers instead of through reimbursements to ensure that taxes are duly withheld and remitted to the national government. We likewise recommended that the required documentary requirements as prescribed above for disbursements made through reimbursements or cash advances shall be observed, otherwise the corresponding disbursements may be suspended or disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant shall strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 15 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184, i.e., to apply the limitation of 25% of basic pay on a monthly basis and not on monthly average based on annualized basic pay of the BAC officials. Moreover, the savings from the procurements should be treated pursuant to the DBM Circular in relation with Section 322 of the Local Government Code.
It is likewise recommended that the concerned officials shall initiate the establishment of separate books and account it on trust with a nil beginning balance commencing the FY 2024 in order to properly account the various transactions of the BAC and to avoid payment of excessive honorarium to the BAC officials.
Moreover, the concerned BAC officials were required to immediately refund in full the excess honorarium paid in the amount of P253,752.75 through the issuance of ND No. 2024-002 dated January 30, 2024.

We recommended that Management ensure that all cash advances, except for travels, shall be made by the accountable officers who are properly designated and properly bonded pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002. No additional cash advances should be granted to those with unliquidated cash advances and that it should be disbursed only for purposes allowed under the said COA Circular. Lastly, the municipal accountant is required to monitor the liquidation of cash advances at the end of the year and ensure that there should be nil amount thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive authorize the AGDB to furnish the Municipal Accountant and the Auditor a copy of the monthly bank statement in accordance with item 3.4 of the COA Circular No. 96-011. Moreover, the Municipal Treasurer shall see to it that monthly bank statements are furnished to the said Offices. Finally, the Municipal Accountant shall ensure that monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement shall be timely prepared and submitted.
We recommended that Management ensure that proper format of PCV will be used in disbursing its Petty Cash Fund in order to trim down the process in the payments of its petty operating expenses.

We recommended that that Bids and Awards Committee ensure all required postings as mentioned in the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 are complied with. Attach a printed copy of all postings to the related disbursement vouchers of these projects to prove compliance thereof.

We recommended that the Management adhere to the provisions prescribed in the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 pertaining to the warranty security, not only for infrastructure projects but also for the procurement of goods. Henceforth, ensure that future procurements be deducted with 1% retention money or require a special bank guarantee of the same amount, otherwise any expenses due to the defects within the warranty period be charged to concerned approving officials.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive, Treasurer, Accountant and Budget Officer refrain from approving/allowing assistance to individuals/organizations that is not within the Municipality’s responsibility to finance and/or subsidized. In the case of organizations conducting activities with a cause/outreach programs it should include as supporting documents as proof that it was used as intended and/or there is an expected beneficial return to the Municipality and its constituents.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant require from the government agencies asking for assistance a certification from their respective authorized officer that said activity has no appropriation and/or not funded by the agency’s fund. Likewise, the amount shall be appropriately acknowledged thru issuance of the agency’s Official Receipt to ensure that such assistance is accordingly recorded in the recipients’ books and will be used as intended.

We recommended that Management ensure that all procurement activities of the Municipality be supported with complete documentation as prescribed. In this regard, the lacking and necessary explanatory documents for the disbursements amounting to P1,998,760.22 be submitted for verification. Should the documents be not submitted or found not in order, the disbursement may be suspended or disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with Section 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to ensure that all unexpended balances in the Trust Fund will be refunded to the source government agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to drop the non-capitalizable PPEs with aggregate cost of ₱1.161 million to correct the balance of the PPE accounts reported in the financial statements in line with Section 27 of IPSAS 1 and Section 111 of P.D. 1445 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer replace the existing ARE/PAR for these items by Inventory Custodian Slips (ICS), except for low valued semi-expendable property with expired estimated useful life, wherein the existing AREs shall be automatically cancelled.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant derecognize the PPE items which are below the capitalization threshold and rectify the misclassification of accounts noted in our audit. Thereafter, recompute the depreciation of PPE items and prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to correct the reported balance of the appropriate Accumulated Depreciation and Prior Period Adjustments/Government Equity accounts in line with Section 27 of IPSAS 1 and Section 111 of P.D. 1445 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of PPE accounts totaling to ₱30.583 million in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.
We recommended that Management consider transferring the PPEs totaling to ₱5.903 million to the recipient government offices by accomplishing the Property Transfer Receipts (PTRs) which will be the basis of the Municipal Accountant in preparing journal entries to drop the items of PPE from the books of accounts of LGU Cabatuan, Isabela and come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.
We recommended to Management the following:
Instruct the Inventory Committee to submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor’s Office within the deadline set by the Commission which is not later than January 31 of each year;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to regularly update the PPELCs and PCs to ensure the integrity of property custodianship and that adequate internal control is adapted in accordance with pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth in COA Circular 2020-006 and properly disclose all the information required in the COA Circular and prepare all the necessary reports thereto.
We recommended that Management monitor and fast-track the registration and transfer of the titles of land in the name of LGU Cabatuan, Isabela to have conclusive evidence of ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in adherence to Section 39(2) of PD No. 1445 and Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of various items of unpaid obligations totaling to ₱5.267 million in line with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry to correct the misclassification of accounts of ₱0.187 million in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information. Moreover, we recommended that Management send notice to the concerned individuals to file their claims by submitting the necessary documents, otherwise, revert to the Government Equity the amount of ₱0.175 million which remained unclaimed for more than five (5) years in line with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management ensure the timely and full implementation of the projects downloaded by national government agencies, cause the immediate liquidation of the funds transferred, and return the unexpended balances to the source agencies upon completion of the projects to fully settle the obligations.  Moreover, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry to correct the misclassification of account amounting to ₱65,100.00 in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information. Moreover, we recommended that Management send notice to the concerned creditors to file their claims by submitting the necessary documents, otherwise, revert to the Government Equity the amount of ₱94,800.00 which remained unclaimed for more than ten (10) years in line with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of accounts totaling to ₱67,465.80 in line with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant identify the cause(s) of the deficiency and immediately coordinate with the GSIS to reconcile and update agency/employee records. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure full and timely deduction and remittance of GSIS contributions of employees in strict adherence to Section 6 of R.A. No. 8291, also known as the Government Service Insurance System Act of 1997.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant identify the cause(s) of the deficiency and immediately coordinate with the PhilHealth to reconcile and update agency/employee records. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure full and timely deduction and remittance of PhilHealth premium contributions of employees in strict adherence to Paragraph 2 of PhilHealth Circular No. 004-2015.

Require the concerned contractor to immediately rectify the deficiency noted in the inspection and conform with the approved program of works by installing the remaining 1-piece 200W spotlight otherwise, refund the equivalent amount of ₱12,285.00 to prevent issuance of Notice of Disallowance (ND) by the COA Audit Team; and
Direct the Municipal Engineer to henceforth conduct regular and strict monitoring and supervision of all local government projects to ensure that these are accomplished in accordance with the approved program of works and completed within their specified contract time to fully achieve the intended benefits of these projects.

Require the Municipal Engineer to submit the Statement of Work Accomplished, as of the date of the expiration of the project, as basis in the imposition and collection of liquidated damages from the concerned contractor in adherence to Section 8.1, Annex E of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184. Moreover, ensure that all government projects are accomplished in accordance with the approved program of works and completed within their specified contract time to fully achieve the intended benefits of these projects.

Regularly monitor and evaluate the balance of the SEF and ensure inclusion of the unexpended balances in the SEF budget for the ensuing year to maximize its utilization in order to benefit the students and schools and enable them to implement other learning activities; and
Give equal priority to the implementation of the construction, repair, and maintenance of school buildings and facilities to benefit the students and ensure that the SEF be allocated only to programs, projects, and activities which are enumerated in DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 series 2017, as revised.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all his collections, as well as all collections turned over to him by the collectors/tellers, with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance with Section 32, Chapter 3, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs to avoid accumulation of huge amount of cash in his custody. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strengthen the internal control in the safekeeping of collections to avoid losses of government funds in view of the Internal Control Standards for the Philippine Public Sector.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to revisit its existing practices and formulate new strategies to improve its collection efficiency rate and performance in line with DOF Department Order No. 006-2015 dated January 20, 2015 and BLGF Unnumbered Memorandum dated February 1, 2023.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to secure the necessary documents to facilitate the payment and release of the retention monies amounting to ₱0.918 million pursuant to pertinent provisions of Section 62 of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. 9184. Any amount due to the LGU by the concerned suppliers/contractors shall be deducted therefrom.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Treasurer, consider the establishment of Change Funds in the MTO and ensure that personal funds were properly segregated from the daily collections of the Collecting Officers to protect the funds of the LGU from the risk of loss and/or misappropriation in line with Sections 123 and 124 of P.D. 1445.

We recommended that Management limit the assignment of essential and regular functions, especially those requiring accountability for government funds, only to permanent employees who are duly and adequately to safeguard public funds and protect the interest of the government.

We recommended that Management adopt the ATM Payroll system in the payment of salaries and wages to casuals and JOs in order to fully protect public funds and minimize operating costs in the processing thereof in conformity with the National Payroll System and henceforth install strong internal control measures to safeguard government funds in view of Sections 123 and 124 of P.D. 1445.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer observe the instructions provided in the abovementioned regulation with regard to the orderly and systematic issuance of accountable forms.

We recommended that Management direct the Liquidating Officer to secure adequate bonding with the Bureau of Treasury to ensure that the LGU is duly and adequately indemnified in the event of embezzlement or loss of funds under the custody of the Liquidating Officer.

We recommended that the LGU strengthen its internal control on the safekeeping of funds by purchasing and installing a safe/vault where the Liquidating Officer may keep the collections pending its deposit to the bank.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to (i) prepare the BRSs for all bank accounts to ensure that the cash in bank ledger balance tallied with their corresponding bank balance; and (ii) exert more effort in determining the nature and details of the difference of ₱926,353.36 as well as the long-outstanding bank reconciling items to facilitate immediate adjustments in the books.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct (a) all accountable officers and employees concerned to ensure liquidation of cash advances within the prescribed period as required by COA Circular No. 97-002; (b) City Accountant, in collaboration with the City Legal Officer, to intensify collection measures or institute appropriate remedy that would require accountable officers to settle or liquidate their past due accounts; and (c) if conditions and circumstances on writing off the accounts are met, require the City Accountant and the Legal Officer to initiate the procedures for the application of request under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the City Treasurer to (a) exert best effort in improving the tax collection efficiency of the City; and (b) issue warrants of levy on real properties of delinquent taxpayers, as directed under Section 258 of R.A. No. 7160, to avoid possible dismissal from the service, as provided in Section 259 of the same law.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the LCE require the (a) Inventory Committee and Acting CGSO Head  to (i) conduct physical inventory of all PPE of the City on or before January 31 of each year, conclude said inventory count for CY 2023 and immediately submit the related reports to the Audit Team;  (ii) reconcile the resultant inventory reports with the accounting records; and (iii) indicate the required information in the RPCPPE;   (b) Acting CGSO Head to (i) maintain complete PPE records such as  PCs for the proper control and monitoring of the agency property and equipment; and (c) City Accountant and Acting CGSO Head to consider the guidelines on one-time cleansing of PPE accounts to render a reliable PPE information.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) responsible officers to (i) observe prudence on project management, implementation, and monitoring of the PPAs to facilitate the transfer of completed projects to the appropriate asset accounts for fair presentation of balances in the financial statements; (ii) coordinate properly with concerned offices to ensure accurate reporting of information on PPAs; (iii) impose liquidated damages on erring contractors with negative slippages on construction or material delays in the performance of contractual obligation; (iv) institute appropriate legal remedy against the contractors with unsettled advance payments; (b) City Planning and Development Coordinator and City Engineer to present to the Audit Team the approved notices to support the projects’ work suspension; and (c) City Accountant to (i) reconstruct the records of account with incomplete information and work closely with responsible officers on the accurate reporting and disclosure of the Advance Payments and CIP accounts; and (ii) cause the immediate submission of unsubmitted DVs under SEF  to the Audit Team.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) should  instruct the City Accountant to (a) prepare the necessary reports for the NHA housing project Phase II, and submit the necessary disbursement reports and documents to COA Office for the audit; (b) notify the payees of the DSWD trust fund, and if efforts would be futile, revert back the fund to the sponsoring agency; (c) immediately revert the unspent ₱3,817,892.07 LGSF-OFA to the Bureau of Treasury and the ₱169,600.00 balance of the bamboo planting project to PENRO.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to properly dispose of the dormant accounts worth ₱1,393,348.11 in accordance with the guidelines in COA Circular No. 2023-008.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) City Treasurer to discontinue the imposition upon taxpayers of unauthorized fees and charges unless proven that the act of collection is supported by an ordinance enacted by the Sanggunian Panlungsod; and (b) City Accountant to revert the accumulated balance of ₱63,917,043.18 to the General Fund for inclusion in the annual budget/supplemental budget for proper appropriation. We also reiterated our recommendation that responsible offices include in their budgets the regular expenditures which they used to charge in the aforementioned Trust Fund accounts.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE): (a) ensure the full or optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund during the budget year; (b) instruct the (i) City Engineer to conduct thorough feasibility or preliminary engineering study and thresh out imminent or possible problems in the implementation of infrastructure projects that are to be included in the 20% Development Fund priority list; and (ii) City Engineer, City Planning and Development Officer and other concerned officials to thoroughly study all feasible solutions in order to successfully complete the renovation of the MVGCCGH  for the immediate benefit of the constituents; (c) require the concerned officers to be prudent in overseeing the timely implementation of PPAs under their respective responsibilities and impose appropriate sanctions upon the contractors who incur breach of contract or agreement; and (d) direct the City Accountant to (i) submit immediately to the Auditor the un-transmitted disbursement vouchers listed in Appendix A to facilitate the audit and determine the validity, completeness and correctness of the transactions pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and (ii) strengthen her review function on the requisite documentation of procurements.
We further recommended that the LCE impose liquidated damages to the contractor computed at ₱8,124,407.75 for failing to commence the construction of the Calle Vicente Flyover and if deemed appropriate, rescind the contract pursuant to Section 68 of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  direct the: (a) CDRRMO, in collaboration with CDRRMC, to (i) identify and incorporate, under a separate caption, in the AIP the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended LDRRMF from previous years to facilitate monitoring and evaluation of the activities of the fund; (ii) properly and effectively identify LDRRMF related PPAs to be undertaken in order to ensure their full implementation and attainment of the expected outcomes; and (iii) update or revise the AIP as necessary to effect the changes in the PPAs to be implemented; (b) CSWDO to submit the distribution list/list of recipients of the food assistance (rice) for the evaluation of Audit Team; and (c)  City Accountant to  strengthen her review function on the requisite documentation of procurements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Local School Board to ensure the optimum utilization of the SEF during the budget year and coordinate with the City Engineer, City Planning and Development Officer and the BAC for the proper planning, timely implementation and monitoring of the PPAs; (b) concerned officers to be prudent in overseeing the timely implementation of PPAs under their respective responsibilities, and impose appropriate sanctions upon the contractors who incurred delay in the project implementation or breach of contract/agreement; and (c) City Accountant to effect the immediate submission to the Auditor of the unsubmitted DVs listed in Appendix B to facilitate the audit and evaluation of the completeness, validity and propriety of the transactions pursuant to  COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the City continue to comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 9003.  We likewise recommended that the LCE direct the CENRO to prioritize and pursue the establishment and operation of the City’s final disposal facility and to implement strategies mentioned in the 10-Year SWMP in order to minimize the payment of tipping fees. We further recommended that the LCE instruct the Barangay Officials, in coordination with CENRO, to consider inclusion of composting operation in the MRFs and ensure that they are fully operating and functioning pursuant to Section 1, Rule XI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to (a) cause the immediate submission of un-transmitted CYs 2021, 2022 and 2023 accounts, records and reports  to the Audit Team; and (b) exert best efforts and plan out efficient ways to reduce, if not completely eliminate, the continuous incurrence of delays in the submission of accounts.  Continuous failure to comply with this recommendation may result to the suspension of payment of salaries of the officials responsible, as well as of the cost of unsubmitted accounts.

We strongly reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the DC to expedite the immediate disposal of unserviceable vehicles to avoid further deterioration and derive better proceeds from sale. We also recommended that the DC visit and follow the guidelines on property management provided under PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 89-296.

We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer continue to comply with the BIR rules and regulations on the proper withholding and timely remittance of taxes on compensation, value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments. We likewise reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) City Treasurer to expedite the processing of disbursements so that taxes withheld for the month will approximate the amount remitted the following month; and (b) City Accountant to exert best effort in tracing the unreconciled amount in the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer continue on the faithful compliance with the rules and regulations governing the withholding and remittance of premium contributions and loan amortizations due to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG Fund.

We reiterated that the LCE direct the (a) accountable officials and employees of the City to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances; and (b) City Accountant, in coordination with City Legal Office, to exhaust all possible means for the collection/recovery of the disallowed transactions amounting to ₱9,216,704.48 from the persons liable therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the MA and Municipal
Treasurer (MT) to continue its faithful compliance with the withholding and full
remittance of the taxes withheld within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the MAO and Municipal
Treasurer’s Office (MTO) to consistently maintain their good practice of religiously
observing the prescribed deadlines on the withholding of premiums/deductions and
the respective remittance thereof to the GSIS in pursuance of the GSIS Act of 1997.

We recommended that the Management continue to adopt the good practice
of promptly remitting premiums, loan amortizations, and other contributions to the
HDMF in compliance with the provisions of R.A. No. 9679.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MAO and MTO
continue to be compliant with regulations on withholding and remittance of monthly
PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period.

We recommended that Management undertake the following measures:
a) require the MT to (i) strictly adhere to the regulations requiring the deposit
of collections daily and intact; (ii) ensure that the entries in the cashbooks
are complete and accurate; and (iii) refrain from using collections for
emergency purchases, vales, and the like; and
b) instruct the MTO and the MAO to reconcile entries in the cashbooks and
accounting ledgers at least on a quarterly basis.

We recommended the management to:
a. instruct the MA to (i) make sure that all ongoing infrastructure projects
are accurately recorded as CIP in the FS as at year-end; and (ii) draw the
JEVs taking up the costs of the ongoing infrastructure projects based on
actual accomplishments;
b. ensure the commitment of the MEO to submit to the MAO a monthly
monitoring report/SWA regarding the status/progress of the construction
of all ongoing projects to facilitate the timely recording of CIP in the books.

We recommended Management to:
a) instruct the MA and GSO to (i) reconcile the accounts of the newly
acquired PPE; and (ii) continue to update the PPELCs for all property
accounts, including their impairment losses, to support the recorded
balances of PPE;
b) direct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count of
PPE; (ii) update/submit the corresponding RPCPPE (with appropriate
remarks on the condition and whereabouts of the PPE) to the MAO, GSO,
and AT; (iii) prepared an IIRUP to all determined unserviceable PPE; and
(iv) assess all PPE for any potential impairment using the indications
enumerated under Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 21;
c) instruct the GSO and other concerned officer to register and insure all the
motor vehicles under the name of the Municipality in the LTO and GSIS,
respectively; and
d) conduct a one-time cleansing of PPE in compliance with the requirements
of COA Circular No. 2020-006 to ascertain the existence, accuracy, and
reliability of the recorded PPE as at year-end.

We recommended that Management:
a) refund the unexpended balances of various fund transfers to its respective
Source Agencies;
b) remit the collected fire code fees to the BTr in compliance with
unnumbered Memorandum of BLGF dated January 18, 2012; and
c) instruct the MA to draw the following accounting entries to correct the
misstatements in Liabilities and Government Equity accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Due to NGAs 19,191.00
Government Equity 19,191.00

We commended Management for continued compliance with the guidelines
for utilization of 20% DF under DBM-DOF-DILG (JMC) No. 1 dated November 04,
2020. However, we still recommended that Management:
a. ensure that the contractor completes the work procured within the period
prescribed by the Procuring Entity as specified in the contract; and
b. instruct the (i) Accounting Office/Unit to scrutinize the validity period of the
performance and warranty security before payment; (ii) Bids and Awards
Committee to (a) compel the contractor to post-warranty security to cover
structural defects after the issuance of final acceptance; and (b) require the

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the LDRRM
Officer to (i) strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs
to achieve the goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits; (ii) include in
the preparation of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund
Investment Plan (MDRRMFIP)/Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the list of PPAs to be
undertaken out of the unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize
the use of the STF; and (iii) submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
LDRRMF within the reglementary period and the Revised/Updated LDRRMFIP to
COA Office for timely review of the report.

We commended the Municipality for its earnest effort in implementing various
programs, projects, and activities for their sustainable SWM. However, we still
recommended to:
a) achieve the full operationalization of the MSWMB to readily act on
emerging issues in the implementation of solid waste management and
provide effective and diverse solutions thereto and by religiously
performing all the duties and responsibilities required of themselves;
b) provide funding for the determined programs, strategies and activities for
SWM or source out financing from other government agencies that could
provide financial assistance to the Municipality;
c) provide regular supplies of Personal Protective Equipment and
appropriate trainings to garbage collectors and other personnel directly
dealing with collection of solid waste, covers to waste compartments of
trucks or haulers and sufficient details for identification on the body of all
motor vehicles used for collection and transport of solid wastes; and
d) encourage and provide support to activate the established Barangay MRFs
and obliged those who does not have to construct their own MRF.

We recommended the LCE to:
a. instruct the MT to (i) prepare a stratified schedule of presumptive income
level every tax period to approximate the gross sales or receipt of each
business classification and submit a representation to the SB to set up or
institutionalize the use of the same; (ii) regularly examine the books of
accounts of business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the
declared gross sales or receipts used in the computation of business taxes
paid pursuant to Section 171 of R.A. No. 7160; (iii) equip the personnel at
the MTO and the BPLS with the necessary knowledge and training in the
examination of books of accounts to enable the said Offices to carry on
with their mandate efficiently; (iv) tag all the afore-cited suppliers and
contractors with underdeclared sales or receipts and be subject to
examination of books of accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees,
and charges to be collected; and (v) require the concerned taxpayers to
pay the business tax deficiency of at least ₱318,425.76;
b. advise the Designated BPLO to require the submission of complete
documentation of the financial records from taxpayers and ensure that
these records form part of their permanent files to easily verify the
correctness of the assessments; and
c. require the MT and the Designated BPLO to devise measures to validate
the declared gross sales or receipts of establishments applying for business
permits especially those who are having direct transactions with the
Municipality and its Barangays.

We recommended that the LCE, being the Chairman of the GFPS, undertake
the following courses of action, through its Focal Person:
a. monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs and evaluate the level and
extent to which the GAD PPAs have become effective and efficient in
addressing GAD issues;
b. instruct the MPDC to facilitate the gathering of data/information needed
for the creation of an updated and well-established GAD database to have
an accurate and reliable basis in the planning and programming of PPAs
as well as in the policy formulation in order to properly address the existing
and emerging GAD issues and concerns;
c. create a GAD M & E Team to establish, enhance, strengthen, and/or
maintain a gender-responsive M & E system; and
d. observe the deadline for submission of GAD-AR to the AT.

We recommended that management instruct the MA and the MT including all
Accountable Officers to (a) immediately transmit to the AT the unsubmitted accounts
and financial reports; and (b) strictly observe the timeframes on the submission of
accounts and financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) continue the enforcement in the settlement of the disallowance of
₱2,663,770.05 in accordance with the payment schedule;
(b) take necessary actions to expedite the collection of the unsettled
disallowances from KASAMA Inc.; and

1.9	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to create an Inventory Committee to spearhead the planning and the conduct of physical count of all PPE of the Municipality and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGU and COA Circular No. 2020-006.
1.10	Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General Services Officer to prepare Property Cards and to reconcile balances thereof with that of the accounting records regularly.
1.11	We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel to exert effort in retrieving reports or documents to identify the properties recorded under the SEF.

2.7	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Disposal and Appraisal Committee to fast track the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties to avoid further deterioration and consequent reduction in their value.
2.8	We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the carrying amount of unserviceable properties after the disposal and to recognize the gain or loss arising therefrom.

3.14	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make the required adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts in the financial statements.
3.15	Moreover, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to henceforward record the purchases of construction materials for development projects by administration as inventory and the consumption thereof in the Construction in Progress account, as well as, to recognize in the appropriate PPE accounts upon completion.

4.7	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the discrepancy of ₱(1.478) million in the cashbook of Cash in Bank- Local Currency, GF accounts.
4.8	We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to regularly reconcile their books to ensure that any possible errors are immediately detected and rectified.

5.7	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to:
Submit the prior authority from the local sanggunian and the specific sources of funds used for the time deposits to establish whether these are essentially idle funds;
Verify the source and purpose of the funds to ascertain the funds’ actual status and nature so that appropriate accounting entries can be prepared in the books of the agency; and
Re-evaluate the placement of time deposits as investment or terminate and transfer these funds to the appropriate bank account to be made available for projects that could immediately benefit constituents.

6.7	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare correcting journal entries to reclassify the advance payments made to contractors to Advances to Contractors account instead of Other Receivables, and henceforth, all other advance payments to be made to contractors.

7.7	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to make adjusting entries to record the proceeds from sale of bidding documents as Trust Liabilities.
7.8	Henceforward, we also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize as revenue any excess amount after the payment of BAC honoraria.

8.10	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to coordinate with all officials concerned before approving variation orders ensuring that any changes to the contract of projects/programs/activities are sufficiently supported with appropriation.
8.11	We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to properly monitor the implementation of the projects/programs/activities and to coordinate with other offices/departments concerned. Effective monitoring and feedback system in the implementation of projects ensure that Management and officials concerned are updated on the status and that corrective measures can be immediately addressed on problems encountered.

9.11	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to collect the liquidated damages from the contractor/supplier totaling ₱64,419.50.
9.12	We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive and other officials concerned agreed to monitor the timely delivery of procured goods in accordance with the conditions stipulated in the contract, otherwise, liquidated damages should be imposed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require personnel hired under Job Order and Contract of Service to comply with the requirements of BIR RMC No. 51-2018, withhold the Percentage Tax from the remuneration received by all personnel hired as JOB/COS, and provide reasonable justification in order for this Office not to disallow the unremitted supposed taxes to be withheld.

We recommended that Management release the 10% Retention Money after the defects liability period and upon final acceptance of the works. In case of substitution, the Municipal Accountant must ensure that the required supporting documents are complete, reviewed and attached to the Disbursement Vouchers. Further, Management is required to abide to the rules and regulations mentioned above to continuously protect the interest of the government.

We recommended Management to (a) ensure that procurement of Fuel, Oil and Lubricant is in conformity with the afore-stated regulations, (b) strictly comply to the procedures and controls in accomplishing the forms and reports in accordance with COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977 prescribing the use of the Manual of Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicle, and (c) enjoin personnel delegated to directly purchase or contract FOL products to submit documents showing proofs of his or her determination of the above-named gasoline stations as supplier to the LGU.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:
a) Ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the various PPAs under the 5% Calamity Fund as planned specifically the Construction of Multi-Purpose Hall/Evacuation Center at Barangay Del Pilar in order to enhance the resiliency and preparedness of the municipality in terms of disaster risk reduction and management, and to strictly observe prompt and timely execution of the said project given the availability of the resources to attain best results and enjoyment of the constituents of the benefits that may be derived from it; and b) Require the concerned employees to prepare and submit the required reports as prescribed in COA Circular 2014-002.

We recommended the Municipal Engineer to submit her written explanation on the change of location, from Barangay Sampaloc to Barangay Tandul, during the implementation of subject project “Vermicomposting and Aquaculture Using Fish Tank” with contract cost of ₱699,528.90. Also, we recommended the Municipal Agriculturist to provide her justification as to the reason of its exclusion of Barangay Tandul in her Project Proposal and finding it more vital to identify Barangay Sampaloc as target beneficiary of the subject project.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer submitted its explanation that originally, the project was proposed on five locations, including Brgys.Sampaloc and Tandul. However, the location for Sampaloc was not ready at that time hence they decided to transfer to Tandul. While they admitted that supplemental document was not provided for the change of project location, there was no change in the construction cost of the project. Lastly, the community benefitted from this project.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of sufficient and relevant documents prior to processing of payments in compliance to Presidential Decree No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended the following:

The Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, being the Implementing Office, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer must submit the required or relevant documents mentioned above to ascertain propriety and validity of the specific transactions, liquidation and distribution and to avoid audit suspension, if warranted.

Responsible official or employee must submit an explanation on non-distribution of the 3,780 cans of meatloaf purchased during the 11th Cycle with total cost of ₱52,920.00 to intended beneficiaries.

Strict compliance of Management on the above-stated rules and regulations and other related issuances in the implementation of the Supplementary Feeding Program to ensure efficient and effective implementation and attainment of its objectives.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (a) issue Certificate of Final Acceptance for completed infrastructure projects after the one-year defects liability period, and after all defects and failures, if any, shall have been repaired by the contractor to the satisfaction of the Project Engineer and (b) require the contractors to post warranty security in the form prescribed in the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to answer for structural defects and/or failures that may occur within the warranty period.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that Petty Cash Expenses should only be used for payment of operating expenses in small amounts which could not be conveniently paid in checks, or which require immediate payment.

We recommended Management that the 20% Development Fund should be optimally utilized in the implementation of priority development projects which are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the (a) City Treasurer to (i) furnish a copy of Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due (CLTTD) to the City Accountant as early as possible; (ii) exert more effort to improve the tax collection efficiency of the City; and (iii) issue warrants of levy on real properties of taxpayers with delinquencies, as directed under Section 254 of R.A. No. 7160; (b) City Accountant to use the CLTTD as the basis in recording the receivables at the beginning of the year; and (c) City Accountant and City Treasurer to reconcile their records of RPT/SET receivables for reliable account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to (a) ensure that recorded obligations are completely supported by proper claim documents; (b) revert to the Unappropriated Surplus of the GF and SEF those against which no actual claim has been filed; and (c) maintain properly the subsidiary ledgers for each creditor.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) (a) consider the revision of policy on the recruitment and employment of Job Order and Contract of Service employees to conform with the applicable provisions of the appurtenant rules and regulations; (b) instruct the HRMO and all Department Heads concerned to observe prudence on the procurement of services of JO/COS by ensuring that only services with exigent demand are to be procured for the completion of identified local projects; and (c) instruct the HRMO to review diligently the employment applications to avoid work duplicity and unnecessary recruitment.
We recommended that the management continue to comply with the guidelines on LDRRMF as prescribed under RA No. 10121 and DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013.

We recommended that the Gender and Development Focal Point System through the GAD Focal Person continue complying with the provision of relevant laws and regulations relative to gender mainstreaming and to deliver more programs that are gender-responsive to all the City’s residents.

We recommended that the LCE (a) instruct the City Health Officer to continue the satisfactory implementation of the COVID-19 vaccination program to mitigate the spread of the virus; and (b) instruct the City Accountant to observe the proper documentation of transactions pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to (a) observe prudence in computing the due tax liabilities and ensure the accurate remittance thereof; (b) make representation with the BIR on the immediate resolution of the over-remittance; and (c) exert more effort in tracing the unreconciled amount in the Trust Fund.

We recommended that the (a) City Accountant and City Treasurer  continuously observe the rules and regulations governing the withholding and remittance of premium contributions and loan amortizations due to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG Fund; and (b) City Accountant and Human Resource Management and Development Officer coordinate with their counterpart from GSIS to properly address the identified concerns such as the alignment and/or conformity of the City’s collection system with the Agencies’ computerized billing system to avoid discrepancies between the City’s collection and Agencies’ billing amount; and (c) City Accountant to reclassify the ₱3,753,554.74 fund  by debiting Due to PhilHealth and crediting Trust Liabilities by the same amount, and maintain ledgers for the different programs for the fund.

We reiterated that the LCE direct the (a) accountable officials and employees of the City to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances; and (b) City Accountant, in coordination with City Legal Office, to exhaust all possible means for the collection/recovery of the disallowed transactions amounting to ₱9,216,704.48 from the persons liable therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to (i) draw JEVs taking up the costs of the ongoing infrastructure projects based on actual accomplishments; and (ii) disclose relevant information in the Notes to FS about the status, progress, and significance of the land titling process for the reclaimed land.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to conduct an in-depth post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification Evaluation Report contains comprehensive results on the verification of technical requirements.

We also recommended that the BAC with the assistance of the TWG include in their post-qualification a detailed and comparative evaluation showing the number of projects with the same key personnel pledged and assigned by the lowest calculated bidder.  Thereafter, require the bidder to commit or assign other qualified personnel to other simultaneous projects to avoid delays in the implementation and substandard quality of work.
observations.managementAction: No management action.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare adjusting entry to recognize the payables in the Trust Fund and Special Education Fund books amounting to ₱168,815.16 and ₱9,542.74, respectively and recognize in the General Fund books the fund transfer (payment from Trust Fund) amounting to ₱832,607.03; and
Trace the prior years’ transactions to establish the correct balances of the intra-agency accounts.
Moreover, we recommended that upon establishment of the correct balances of the intra-agency accounts, the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the actual cash to the debtor fund so that implementation of programs/projects/activities will not be hampered.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to establish the correct PPE balances and corresponding depreciation; and
Maintain PPE Ledger Cards for all PPE accounts with complete details on name of project and location in order to monitor the additions of PPEs to support the accuracy and existence of PPEs.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the GSO-Designate in tracing the differences between the Report on Physcial Count of Property, Plant and Equipment and accounting records.
Also, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to enjoin the Inventory Committee, GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant to undertake the procedural guidelines enumerated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to come up with more reliable account balances to be presented in the financial statements.
We also recommended and the Disposal Committee agreed to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties to free up storage spaces, prevent further deterioration of PPE and generate additional income from sale.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS 2, 17 and COA Circular No. 2015-008 by adequately providing the:
Amount and nature of restricted cash balances;
Useful lives or depreciation rates used the carrying amount of temporarily idle PPE, and the gross carrying amount of any fully depreciated PPE that is still in use; and
Length of roads with corresponding total cost, additions, major repairs and regular maintenance incurred, reductions through derecognition and impairment and impairment reversed.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Trace the transactions pertaining to “Other Reconciling Items” totaling to ₱1,931,628.07; and
Maintain Registries of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAOs) as her basis in the preparation of SCBAA which is to be reconciled with the Status of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (SAAO) of the Municipal Budget Officer.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Budget Officer to use proper account titles in the preparation of Annual and Supplemental Budget in order to reflect accurate and truthful information in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:
Require the supplier to submit the appropriate Income Tax Return and the complete Omnibus Sworn Statement for all the procured welfare goods awarded with ABC above ₱500,000.00; and
Ensure that all emergency procurements are awarded to a technically, legally, and financially capable supplier, contractor, distributor, or consultant in compliance with the aforementioned provisions.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MDRRMO, with the help of Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal Budget Officer, to prepare its original and supplemental LDRRMFIP duly approved by the Sanggunian to clearly elaborate the details of its DRRM program for proper accounting and monitoring of the calamity fund, classifying the PPAs whether they are MOOE or Capital Outlay.
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
The Municipal Accountant to ensure that unexpended balance of the LDRRMF – MOOE and QRF are recognized as Trust Liability- LDRRMF at year-end; and
The Municipal Treasurer to ensure actual cash thereof is transferred to a special trust fund at the end of the year pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121 to avoid misuse of funds which must be solely disbursed for disaster risk reduction and management activities.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the BAC and TWG to:
Monitor each procurement activities specified in the Annual Procurement Plan and reflect the information in the Procurement Monitoring Report as well as the timeline on all procurement activities; and
Prepare and submit the required Revised Annual Procurement Plan and Procurement Monitoring Report within the prescribed period to the GPPB.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the BAC and the TWG to conduct an in-depth post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification Evaluation Report contains comprehensive results on the verification of technical requirements.
Also, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the BAC to check carefully the necessary documents before entering into Contract Agreements and follow strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 in order to attain competitive bidding and to guarantee that the programs and projects in the municipality may not be put at risk during the implementation of the infrastructure projects and delivery of goods.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to harmonize the grant of discount and penalty rates provided in the Provincial Ordinance No. 115-2017 and other issuances of the Province of La Union pertaining to the imposition and collection of real property taxes.
