The GSO Designate to cancel the ARE related to the unserviceable properties turned over to him;
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive create a Disposal Committee.  Thereafter, require the Committee to prepare an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and facilitate proper disposal of unserviceable properties to avoid further deterioration due to natural elements and possible loss due to theft and loss of value, generate additional funds, if found valuable and sold.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to make a representation with the Provincial Treasurer’s Office to seek assistance in the enforcement of collection of delinquent taxes, especially those long over-due.  Moreover, to ask assistance from the Barangay Officials for them to inform the heirs of the deceased delinquent taxpayers or occupants of the properties to pay their delinquent taxes.

We recommended and Management agreed to come-up with better strategy on how the market will be viable to help the local investors. Also, seek prospective stallholders for the nine (9) vacant stalls in order to generate additional income that could be used for other developmental projects.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct:
The City Treasurer:
introduce collection strategy that would significantly help increase the RPT/SEF  tax collection of the City;
coordinate with the City Accounting Office for the reconciliation of their respective records and to effect the necessary adjustments/corrections;
timely prepare an updated Certified List of Taxpayers, bearing the names, particulars of the property, location, amount due and collectible for the applicable year and other pertinent information; and submit the same to the accounting unit as the basis in setting up of RPT/SEF tax receivable in the books of accounts of the City at the beginning of the year;
ensure that the list of taxpayers bear vital information, such as the exact addresses, to facilitate, if necessary, the sending of notices and other follow up communications relative to tax campaign activities and other collection strategy that may be  implemented by Management; and
use available remedies, administrative and/ or judicial action, for the collection of taxes as provided in Section 256 of the Local Government Code of 1991.
The  City Accountant:
observe the provision under Section 20 of the New Government Accounting (NGAS) Manual by using the Certified List of Taxpayers and Tax Due prepared by the Office of the City Treasurer as the basis in recording the RPT/SEF tax receivables at the beginning of the year; and
coordinate with the City Treasurer’s Office for the reconciliation of their respective records and to effect the necessary adjustments/corrections in the reported balances of the affected accounts, if any.
The City Assessor to coordinate and timely provide the CTO with the needed assessment reports to facilitate the preparation of Certified List of Taxpayers by the latter within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the –
City General Services Officer (CGSO) to:
strictly comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated PPELC and PC, respectively for each class of property and to record the acquisition, adjustments and disposal thereof;
observe the annual submission of the physical inventory reports on PPEs to the Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to existing rules and regulations for timely review and validation.
Inventory Committee in coordination with CGSO, exert extra effort to complete the physical inventory of all PPEs of the City Government and reconcile the report with the accounting records; and
City Accountant to coordinate with the CGSO and Inventory Committee for the reconciliation of respective records and effect the necessary corrections/adjustments, if warranted, in the books of accounts.

In line with the observed condition and the fact that recommendations for the immediate disposal of subject unserviceable PPEs were consistently incorporated in the previous years’ annual audit reports due to non-implementation by the agency,  it is important to remind the management that the continued non-disposal of these assets was inconsistent with Section 79 of the above-mentioned PD No. 1445.
The initial action by management, thru the GSO and the Inventory Committee, by submitting the aforementioned IIRUP dated September 30, 2019 together with a letter request for disposal of items listed therein is an indication of compliance with regulations. Subject request by management was favourably indorsed by this Audit Team to the COA Regional Office No. III for technical inspection and evaluation on December 03, 2019. This could pave way for the processing of disposal of subject property which was unacted by the GSO and other Offices concerned for a long period.
We reiterated our recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to  instruct the Head of the General Services Office and the Inventory Committee to (a) cause the immediate correction and completion of required data in  the updated Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP); (b) coordinate with the accounting office for reconciliation of their respective records and effect the necessary adjustments, if any, in the books of the agency; (c) prepare/complete the corresponding assessment, evaluation and appraisal reports; (d) once the request for disposal is approved by COA,  undertake prompt disposal of the subject properties to prevent further deterioration and decline of value in order to immediately generate additional funds from sale thereof; and (e) for the succeeding years, ensure submission to the Office of the LCE, for approval, the schedule of disposal of unserviceable assets and those beyond economical repairs at least yearly, to prevent   the recurrence of similar lapses.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that:
the City Administrator, City Accountant and City Legal Office exhaust all possible means for the liquidation and/or recovery of the ₱8,879,373.13 outstanding cash advances.
The  City Accountant:
ensure timely submission of the liquidation reports and/or settlement of the same to the Office of the COA Auditor for review. The required  supporting documents must be attached to the liquidation reports in accordance with COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and
endeavor to strengthen the internal control system for cash advances, or for all other assets of the City for that matter, to assure that cash advances are liquidated or reported on as soon as the purpose of the same is served, in compliance with Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular 97-002, to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Treasurer, City Accountant and the liquidating officer continue to observe the provisions of Section 181 (c) of GAAM, Vol. I and Section 5.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to safeguard the cash and accountabilities in a diligent manner and to regularly reconcile the treasury and accounting records.

We recommended and management agreed that the City Treasurer, City Accountant and the liquidating officer continue to observe the provisions of Section 181 (c) of GAAM, Vol. I and Section 5.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to safeguard the cash and accountabilities in a diligent manner and to regularly reconcile the treasury and accounting records.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a) Accountable Officer  (i) strictly adhere with the specific provisions cited under COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances;  and (ii) exercise due diligence in recording  all transactions and maintenance of cashbooks by ensuring that daily balances reconciled with the cash on hand (if any),  balances be ruled and foot  per cashbooks at the end of each month, and the required certification thereat immediately below the monthly totals and balances be accomplished ; and  (b) Accountable Officer (Cashier/DO) and the Accountant consistently observe the conduct of regular reconciliation of their records quarterly to timely effect the necessary adjustments,   if  any,  to  avoid  the  accumulation  of reconciling  items  in  their respective records/books.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to  direct :
the Accounting and Treasury Offices to strictly observe the rules and regulations governing the withholding and remittance of taxes, premium contributions, etc. with the BIR, GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG;
the City Treasurer and City Accountant to regularly coordinate in the  processing of documents to facilitate timely transmission of disbursement vouchers and recording of transactions by the respective Offices concerned to ensure that the amount withheld will be remitted intact to the BIR, GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG; and
the Accounting and Treasury Offices exert extra effort in identifying the nature and details of the previous years’ forwarded balances due to GSIS and PhilHealth and make the necessary adjustment thereof to facilitate the immediate remittance of the same to the concerned agencies.

We reiterated our recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to-
instruct the City Accountant to initiate thorough review and trace the original documents and recordings of non-moving old account balances and effect the necessary adjustments/corrections, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts in the books of the agency;
remind the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to cause the immediate return  of the unutilized and/ or non- moving funds to the respective source  Agencies (NGAs) in observance of Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated  December 13, 1994; and
require the Heads of the Offices concerned to strictly observe the time frame of scheduled implementation of the projects to ensure efficient delivery of public services to the intended beneficiaries and to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.

We recommended that the LDRRMO follow strictly the rules on utilization of LDRRMF as stated in DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and refrain from using LDRRMF for programs/projects/activities which are not enumerated among the eligible expenditures authorized in the aforesaid Circular.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to continue the inclusion and prioritization of high-impact developmental projects in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) of the City Government.
We further recommended and management agreed that the LCE direct the -
City Planning Officer to:
strictly monitor the status of implementation of all projects to ensure its  completion within the contract period;
ensure that the projects have been studied  thoroughly including the requirements of securing  Permit to Construct from the Department of Health (DOH), road right of way and property boundary dispute, among others to avoid delay in the completion of projects; and
submit explanation behind the agency’s implementation of  various road concreting  projects thru awarding of contracts to private contractors despite the availability of heavy equipment and other construction machineries and vehicles owned by the City Government which can be utilized for the purpose.
City Engineer to:
closely monitor/supervise the progress of construction to ensure completion of the projects within the contract time for the immediate utilization of the intended beneficiaries; and
compute for liquidated damages, if any, in coordination with the Accounting Unit, of every projects with unjustified delay in the completion and charge the amount to the respective claims of the contractors concerned accordingly.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the Chairman of the BAC and the BAC Secretariat to adhere strictly with the provisions of Sections 13.1 and 13.3 of RA No. 9184 on the timely transmittal of invitation to the Office of the Auditor and other Observers for all scheduled stages of procurement processes to be implemented by the Agency. Further, ensure that the same are duly supported with copies of Program of Works (POW) and Detailed Engineering Estimates, Purchase Request, as applicable, consistent with the enhancement of transparency in government transactions.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct:
The City Engineering Office to:
strictly monitor the work progress of the contractor, regularly conduct physical inspection to ensure timely completion of the project in accordance with the period stipulated in the contract;
make assessment and evaluation of the overall condition/situation of the project site and formulate corresponding remedial measures that may be applied to counter and or solve the possible obstacles that may be encountered in the process. Aforesaid activities may be applied in the implementation of succeeding phases III, IV and V of the NHA housing project in order to prevent the recurrence of similar obstacles that hindered the timely completion of the Phase I project;
require the contractor to immediate effect the completion of the remaining unfinished works, such as the installation of doors and windows on several housing units; and
compute the liquidated damages corresponding to the delayed completion of the project and coordinate the same to the Accounting Office for proper charging against the claim of the contractor.
The Bids and Awards Committee to submit explanation behind the publication of invitation to bid for the construction of the 96 NHA housing units that led to the awarding of the contract to a private contractor. The foregoing action was inconsistent with the provision of the MOA between the NHA and the City which requires that the project is to be implemented by administration by the City under the NHA’s Resettlement Assistance Program to Local Government Units (RAP-LGU).
The Urban Poor Assistance Office (UPAO) to:
facilitate the conduct of activities being required in the approved Resettlement and Relocation Action Plan (RRAP) for the processing of the 96 beneficiaries under the subject housing project;
undertake strict selection and screening of beneficiaries base on set criteria, qualifications and requirements to ensure that the government resources allocated to the project will properly serve its intended purpose; and
expedite other required processes and documentation that will pave way to the realization of the awarding of housing units to the qualified constituents of the City.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive directed -
The City Engineer to:
submit the copy of approved Permit to Construct (PTC) issued by DOH Regional Director and a copy of the Request for Extension by the contractor duly approved by the Head of the Agency;
observe the imposition of liquidated damages for unjustified delay in the completion of project, if warranted, to protect the interest of the City Government;
ensure that, in similar future undertaking,  the necessary approval on hospital construction plan and Permit to Construct (PTC) be secured first from DOH Office before the commencement of construction works to avoid the undue delay in the completion of the project; and
remind the monitoring team to strictly observe regular inspection of projects to ensure reliability of the structure’s compliance with the approved specifications and completion of project within the contract period.
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to be more prudent in planning the procurement activities, review and evaluation to ascertain the completeness of required documents at the early stage of the procurement proceedings.
The City Accountant to submit to this Office the disbursement voucher for the final payment made, if any, for the remaining 10% portion of Phase II completed project, together with the complete supporting documents.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive directed the –
City Accountant and the CGSO to effect the immediate submission of the following documents:
Certification from Barangay Officials concerned, on the areas covered by the contract, attesting that hauling services were actually rendered/took place; Certification should contain, date, time, plate no. of the truck used, number of paleros and number of times of actual hauling per day; and
Accomplishment Report submitted by Unicorn duly validated by a designated officials/ employees at the General Services Office (GSO).
Environmental Protection Division (EPD) and CGSO to observe close monitoring on the compliance of the contractor in the performance of their responsibilities/commitments stipulated in the contract. Report violations committed, if any, and deduct the corresponding penalties from the claim in accordance with Sections 7.3 and 7.4 of the contract.

We recommended and management agreed to exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances. We further recommended that management cause the total settlement of the PEI overpayment.

We recommended and management agreed that the GAD Focal Person continue to (i) develop and implement more GAD related programs/activities that advocate women empowerment, promote gender equality and improve the welfare and protection of rights of women; and (ii) strictly adhere to PCW-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-01 in the implementation of planned projects and maximum utilization of appropriated funds for the purpose.

City Accountant to (i) observe the provisions of Section 20 of the New Government Accounting (NGAS) Manual; and

observations.recommendation:
The Local Chief Executive:
create an inventory committee to conduct physical inventory of all Property, Plant and Equipment of the municipality in order to determine their existence and condition, and
create an inspectorate team that may include representatives from offices of Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Engineer to inspect procured goods and infrastructures.
The Property Officer Designate:
maintain property cards per category of PPE,
post on a prominent but secure portion on the property the property tags for of the municipality for easy identification,
issue ARE to all property accountable officials and employees and keep copies thereof, and
prepare Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.
The Accounting unit:
exert effort to trace back prior years’ accounting records pertaining the properties of the municipality and maintain complete property ledger cards, and
disclose in the notes to financial the necessary information that may be relevant for decision-making of stakeholders.
The Property Officer Designate and Accounting unit:
effect the proper documentation all properties and strengthen internal control in managing and recording of properties, and
exert effort in reconciling their records.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
OIC – Municipal Accountant to locate the documents pertaining to the loans granted as recorded in the Due from Non-Government Organizations/People’s Organizations account; and
Municipal Treasurer to provide the OIC-Municipal Accountant copies of their records and documents pertaining to the account Due from NGOs/POs.
Thereafter require the Accounting and Treasury Office to reconcile their records and make the necessary adjustments if needed.
We further, recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Treasury Office to prepare and issue demand letters/confirmation letters to concerned NGOs/POs, so that funds collected therefrom could be used to other beneficial projects.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned officials to include required data in the Memorandum of Agreement and impose to recipient NGOs/POs maintenance of financial and accounting records as provided under COA Circular No. 2007-001. We also recommend close monitoring and supervision of the livelihood projects implemented by the recipient NGOs/POs to ensure compliance to the Memorandum of Agreement and efficient and effective implementation approved project.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Concerned officials and employees to inform the OIC – Municipal Accountant and the Supply Officer – Designate of the donations received from various agencies in order that the transactions related thereto will be recorded in the books of accounts.
OIC – Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entry to account for the above-mentioned donations in-kind from DOH using the fair value.

With the foregoing observations, we have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The OIC-Municipal Accountant to record in the appropriate inventory account all purchases of inventories upon acceptance of the delivered items (except those purchase out of the Petty Cash Fund); to record expense only when the said inventories were issued to end users and supported by Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS); and to maintain the required Subsidiary Ledger Cards for each item of the inventory and record issuances to end-users upon submission by the Supply Officer Designate.
The Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory within the prescribed frequency and submit the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year;
The Supply Officer-Designate to maintain the appropriate Stock Cards for each item of stock/inventory and regularly prepare the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued based on the approved Requisition and Issue Slips and submit the same to the accounting unit for the proper recording in the book; and
The Supply Officer-Designate and OIC – Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records.

We have recommended that the OIC – Municipal Accountant review the records and transactions pertaining to the Accounts Payable and Due to Officers and Employees accounts to reconcile the discrepancies noted in order to fairly present the said accounts.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the drivers concerned to accomplish properly the serially-numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets in conformity to pertinent laws, rules and regulations. Likewise, for the Property Officer-Designate to prepare and submit within the prescribed period the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption of government motor transportation in order to closely monitor the regularity of fuel consumption.

We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant comply strictly with the submission requirement of the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements for all funds to the Office of the Auditor pursuant to Section 74 of PD 1445 and Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 to facilitate the timely verification of the correctness of the account balances and to avoid the sanctions stated on Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code.

We have recommended that management follow-up the review of Environmental Management Bureau on the submitted Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan particularly on the proposed a sanitary landfill in order to immediately address the deficiencies noted, if any, and implement the adoption of engineered sanitary landfill to strengthen the solid waste management of the Municipality.  We also recommend that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board enjoin the 17 barangays to strictly implement segregation of wastes in order to secure continuation of MOA with Municipality of Agoo and ensure the protection of public health and environment.

We have recommended that casual or permanent employees personnel be assigned to regular functions of the agency and be bonded accordingly with the Bureau of Treasury to insure the government for unrelieved accountabilities of the bonded officials or employees. Thereafter, the Management strictly comply to the provisions set forth by CSC, COA and DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

We have recommended that Management ensure availability of resources and inclusion of the lacking items in the formulation of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan in compliance with the minimum preparedness standards set forth by National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC) to strengthen a safer, adaptive and disaster-resilient Filipino community towards sustainable development.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to immediately  submit the required documents for review and validation.  Thereafter, make certain that payment be only made to transactions with complete supporting documents.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the concerned employees to assign reference numbers of Disbursement Vouchers and Liquidation Reports within the prescribed format and submit the lacking documentary requirements in order to ascertain the validity and regularity of the transactions.

We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant submit immediately the Disbursement Vouchers to the Office of the Auditor to facilitate the timely review and evaluation of the transactions, and avoid administrative disciplinary action.

We recommended the OIC - Municipal Accountant verify the journal entries made to determine the discrepancies between the intra-agency accounts. Also reconcile these reciprocal accounts and make necessary adjusting entry for a fair presentation in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The OIC - Municipal Accountant agreed to check on the journal entries made to determine the discrepancies and reconcile the said accounts.
observations.reason: Adjusting entries were effected, however, the intra-agency accounts were still not reconciled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer make certain that all collections are regularly deposited intact with the authorized depository bank not later than the next banking day in order to deter possible loss or misuse.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer reasoned out that most of the collections indicated as undeposited for more than five working days were collections of the Barangay Treasurers for Community Tax Certificates which were not remitted to her on time. Other collections not deposited within the prescribed period were due to lack of personnel to make the deposits. Nevertheless, she assured that they will strictly adhere to the provisions of the MNGAS to avoid exposing government funds to possible loss or misuse.
observations.reason: Some collections were still not remitted within prescribed period.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer informed the audit team that repair/ rectifications were already made by the contractor, particularly on the longitudinal cracks along Bacungan Street. Repairs on the said road were undertaken to conform to the approved design, plans and specifications with the end of making this road in good condition.
observations.reason: Monitoring of the projects was conducted, however, not all documentary requirements were   submitted and warranty securities after the defects liability period were not posted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units in recording and reporting financial transactions of the municipality as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant guaranteed to strictly comply with our audit recommendation. She agreed to classify the payments to volunteer teachers as Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management cause strict compliance to COA Circular 2009-1 on the timely submission of contract with all its supporting documents by the BAC Chairperson/Secretariat to the Auditor within five (5) days upon execution/ issuance of the contracts

observations.reason: As per records, the management has not submitted all the Purchase Orders and Contracts with its corresponding supporting documents within the prescribed period.
